title: MLOps
description: This package covers essential MLOps concepts, focusing on business expectations, feature stores, and the data assessment layers (Bronze, Silver, Gold) used in ML development.

concepts.title: Business Expectations in ML
concepts.summary: Business expectations involve defining operational rules, such as checking for null values in specific fields or analyzing account activity patterns to detect system problems.

grounding.text:
01: Model Development

Nuno Rosa nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt Last week recap

Basis Expectations:

- Null Values
- Metrics based on Business (such as negative values)

Basis Expectations:

·

Basis Expectations:

...

Possible business expectations:

- When the send_id is null the type of operation is always deposit-cash?
- When there is withdrawal, there is no beneficiary account.
- Percentage of sender account filling over the time vs beneficiary account (can reveal processing problems on the system).

01: Model Development Feature Stores

- Feature stores are repositories of different features associated with business entities that are created and stored in a central location for easier reuse.
- They usually combine an offline part (slower, but potentially more powerful) and an online part (quicker and more useful for real-time needs).

Challenges

1. Amount of data
2. Creating features from  raw  data  (ineffective,  non-standard  and  expensive  feature engineering)
3. Training the model
4. Making  predictions  (inference)  on  new  data.  In  large  organizations,  these  phases  can span different teams

Advantages

1. A  data  warehouse  for  machine  learning  features: the  discovery,  monitoring, analysis, and reuse of features within an organization.
2. Management, access and creation of point-in-time datasets from historical feature data.
3. An  operational  store  for  precomputed  features  that  online  models  can  use  to augment their feature vectors with fea
grounding.citation: 01: Model Development > Basis Expectations: > Possible business expectations: > 01: Model Development Feature Stores > Challenges > Advantages > 01: Model Devel

concepts.title: Feature Store Components and Functions
concepts.summary: Feature stores manage features within Feature Groups, allowing data scientists to generate training data and ensuring real-time models receive current data for applications like fraud detection.

concepts.title: Data Assessment Layers (Bronze, Silver, Gold)
concepts.summary: Data assessment involves multiple layers (Bronze for ingestion, Silver for feature engineering, Gold for ML/Business) to ensure data quality and readiness.

concepts.title: Feature Store Architecture (Medallion)
concepts.summary: The Feature Store integrates with the Gold layer, utilizing an Offline Store for historical batch training and an Online Store for low-latency, real-time predictions.

concepts.title: Feature Management
concepts.summary: Features should be treated as first-class citizens, meaning they are stored centrally with metadata, lineage, and freshness information to prevent training-serving skew.

concepts.title: Feature Definition Components
concepts.summary: Features are defined by several components including their source, name, data type, and descriptive metadata provided by data scientists.

grounding.text:
01: Model Development Feature Management

- o Source -Where the data comes from (table or method of computation).
- o Name -The name of the feature on the data source (e.g. table column), with a well-defined nomenclature.
- o Type -Int, Float, String allows for enforcing the correct behavior in filters and transformations.
- o Description  and  comments  added  by  the  Data  Scientists  to  keep  track  of  insights  about  the  feature,  meaning  of certain values (or where to find it). The impacts on the models, importance and metric summaries.
- o Some statistical measures about the features (cardinality, range).

01: Model Development Feature Management

Every feature is generated using na universal process

01: Model Development Feature Management

- Create a dictionary telling what type of feature is: if it is a natural feature of the event, or a result of some aggregation.
- Create a dictionary with the recipe of how a feature should be calculated.

01: Model Development Feature Management

- The idea is just with one line create the feature using all the define methods in the class

The Feature Execution Pipeline

- Ingestion: System receives event & queries the Feature Dictionary to resolve the taxonomy (Natural vs. Aggregated).
- Smart Caching: For aggregations, check the Feature Store cache first. A cache hit bypasses expensive compute, minimizing latency.
- Standardized Compute: On a cache miss, dynamically generate code using OOP recipes (Configuration over Code
grounding.citation: 01: Model Development Feature Management > The Feature Execution Pipeline > 01: Model Development Feature Engineering > · Old fashioned ones > 01: Model Develop

concepts.title: Impact of Feature Count on MLOps
concepts.summary: Increasing the number of features can lead to higher computational costs, increased maintenance, reduced stability, and privacy concerns.

concepts.title: MLOps Tool Integration
concepts.summary: MLOps practices benefit from integration with popular tools like Python for development and CI/CD pipelines for automation and auditing.

grounding.text:
Integration with Popular Tools

- Python: most used programming language in Machine Learning.
- Easy to integrate in exploring Jupyter Notebooks.

Reproducibility and Auditing

- Integration with CI/CD pipelines.
- Capturing the environment (dependencies, libraries, and system configurations)

Collaboration and Knowledge Sharing

- Flexibility and scalability for real-time predictions, and batch predictions.
- Integration on local serving or cloud platforms.
- Visualizations to facilitate communication even for non-technical stakeholders.
- Teams can easily share experiments links, review and discuss.
- Auditors can verify and understand the results.

01: Model Development

- Power of abstraction.
- It packages and modularizes the models
- UI for easier management of all MLFlow key features.

01: Model Development

- Creating experiments: Wecan group our models and relevant metrics. Each experiment can have several runs.

01: Model Development

- Creating experiments: Wecan group our models and relevant metrics. Each experiment can have several runs.
- It is easier to keep track of each run of our model.
- Wecan reproduce the results since the parameters are saved there.

01: Model Development

- Model and metrics logging: Wecan save a model in a modular form and log all of the metrics related to the model run (training, validation and testing). For each run you can decide which metrics you want to save, plots, graphs, confusion matrices, artifacts.

01: Model Development

-

concepts.title: MLOps Accomplishments
concepts.summary: The session covered key areas of MLOps, including dependency management, data management, model management, deployment, and experimentation management.

grounding.text:
What did we accomplish today?

- Dependency management: Reproducibility and library conflicts exclusion between different environments.
- Data management: Full knowledge of what enters in the model.
- Model management: Models organized in a logic of several challengers development vs champion with a certain abstraction level and standardization.
- Deployment: Smooth deployment, with agnostic model production.
- Experimentation Management: Parameters are stored in the same way -> reproducibility of the experiment.
grounding.citation: What did we accomplish today?

concepts.title: Reasons for MLOps
concepts.summary: MLOps is necessary because the rate of success and investment in machine learning is not high, and the tangible benefits of ML projects can diminish.

grounding.text:
Introduction: Fundamentals

Nuno Rosa (Spring 2026) nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt

Why MLOps? Machine Learning Failures

Total disclosed value of equity investments in privately held AI companies, by target region

Why MLOps? Machine Learning Failures

- The rate of success and investment on machine learning was not so high.
- Tangible benefits associated with machine learning projects start to wane.

concepts.title: Need for MLOps
concepts.summary: ML models are not static in the real world due to changing external factors like data and business needs, requiring a solid process for deployment and maintenance.

grounding.text:
Why MLOps?

A model can be highly performant in our jupyter notebook!!! When goes to the real world, this process will not be static . There are many external factors to the model, which makes imperative to have a solid process of model deploy and maintenence.

Painful real world -> business + data + model

1. Data is constantly changing . Worse than data, is business, whose needs shift as well: strong dependence on the alignment between the productions results and the original goal.
2. Comunication : a model is a "baby" breed by Data Scientists, neverthless the family is heterogenous. The baby is educated and raised by business teams, IT teams, and non of these use the same tools and share the same knowledge background.
3. Data Scientists are not software engineers : a smooth deploy and machine learning life cycle should be based on the development of applications fully tested and that take into account the scalability of several machine learning models.

Painful real world -> business + data + model

A mature organization that start to depends on the results of machine learning initiatives, should evolve to something that does not depend on the figure of full-stack data scientists!

What MLOps?

ML + DEV + OPS

What MLOps?

- The challenges of ML model operationalization have a lot in common with software productionisation where DevOps has proven itself.
- Best practices from DevOps is a prudent approach to help data scientists overcome challenges common to software product
grounding.citation: Why MLOps? > Painful real world -> business + data + model > What MLOps? > Problems: > 1. Reproducibility > 2. Modularity > 3. Model Versioning > 4. Unit and In

concepts.summary: Key problems in ML include lack of version control for data/models/dependencies, hardcoded paths, lack of reproducibility, insufficient logging/experiment tracking, and poor modularity/testing.

concepts.title: MLOps Practices
concepts.summary: Core MLOps practices include ensuring reproducibility through versioned data/models and random seed control, achieving modularity by splitting code into components, tracking models via versioning, and implementing unit/integration tests.

concepts.title: Modularity in MLOps
concepts.summary: Modularity involves breaking down large ML workflows into independent, sequential steps (like Ingestion, Cleaning, Features, Training) which allows for smart caching and parallel team work.

grounding.text:
With modularity:

- Break massive notebooks into independent, sequential steps (Ingestion Cleaning Features Training).
- The pipeline remembers previous steps. Changing a single model parameter only reruns the training node, cutting experiment time from hours to minutes (smart caching).
- Because components are separated by standardized inputs and outputs, multiple teams can work on different nodes simultaneously without causing code conflicts.

What MLOps? Real world example

The Scenario: A regional bank has a machine learning model that predicts whether a customer will default on a loan. Initially, it is managed by two data scientists and trained on a clean, static CSV file. To scale , the bank wants to roll this model out nationally, pulling live data from 50 different local branch databases, updated hourly.

With unit tests:

- The data pipeline runs unit tests on every batch of incoming data.
- The $50,000 string, flags a schema violation, blocks that specific batch from entering the feature store.
- Alerts the data engineering team.

concepts.title: MLOps Definition and Principles
concepts.summary: MLOps adapts DevOps principles to Machine Learning Operations, focusing on continuous integration, delivery, and managing the unique challenges introduced by data.

grounding.text:
Quantitative risk analysis for decision makers

A: The Budget Pipeline, Architecture / Pipeline Design = 5-minute Micro- Batches, Static features (Yesterday's data). A: The Budget Pipeline, Operation al Cost (OpEx) = $5,000 (Low Compute). A: The Budget Pipeline, Risk Cost (Fraud Losses) = $400,000 (High Fraud). A: The Budget Pipeline, Total System Cost = $405,000. A: The Budget Pipeline, Verdict = Unacceptable Bleeding money to fraud.. B: The Fortress Pipeline, Architecture / Pipeline Design = 100% Real-time Deep Learning, 3 external API calls per transaction. B: The Fortress Pipeline, Operation al Cost (OpEx) = $350,000 (Massive Compute). B: The Fortress Pipeline, Risk Cost (Fraud Losses) = $5,000 (Near Zero Fraud). B: The Fortress Pipeline, Total System Cost = $355,000. B: The Fortress Pipeline, Verdict = Unacceptable Over- engineered; costs more to run than fraud itself.. C: The Cascade (Sweet Spot), Architecture / Pipeline Design = 95% fast Rule- Engine/LightGBM 5% routed to Deep Learning. C: The Cascade (Sweet Spot), Operation al Cost (OpEx) = $45,000 (Optimized Compute). C: The Cascade (Sweet Spot), Risk Cost (Fraud Losses) = $25,000 (Managed Risk). C: The Cascade (Sweet Spot), Total System Cost = $70,000. C: The Cascade (Sweet Spot), Verdict = Success Decouples high throughput from high compute costs.

concepts.title: Pipeline Design Trade-offs
concepts.summary: Different pipeline designs (Budget, Fortress, Cascade) involve trade-offs between operational costs (OpEx), risk costs (fraud losses), and overall system cost.

grounding.text:
Quantitative risk analysis for decision makers

A: The Budget Pipeline, Architecture / Pipeline Design = 5-minute Micro- Batches, Static features (Yesterday's data). A: The Budget Pipeline, Operation al Cost (OpEx) = $5,000 (Low Compute). A: The Budget Pipeline, Risk Cost (Fraud Losses) = $400,000 (High Fraud). A: The Budget Pipeline, Total System Cost = $405,000. A: The Budget Pipeline, Verdict = Unacceptable Bleeding money to fraud.. B: The Fortress Pipeline, Architecture / Pipeline Design = 100% Real-time Deep Learning, 3 external API calls per transaction. B: The Fortress Pipeline, Operation al Cost (OpEx) = $350,000 (Massive Compute). B: The Fortress Pipeline, Risk Cost (Fraud Losses) = $5,000 (Near Zero Fraud). B: The Fortress Pipeline, Total System Cost = $355,000. B: The Fortress Pipeline, Verdict = Unacceptable Over- engineered; costs more to run than fraud itself.. C: The Cascade (Sweet Spot), Architecture / Pipeline Design = 95% fast Rule- Engine/LightGBM 5% routed to Deep Learning. concepts.title: Software Evolution Stages
concepts.summary: Software development has evolved from traditional code-based logic (Software 1.0) to data-driven models (Software 2.0) and now to autonomous, prompt-driven systems (Software 3.0).

grounding.text:
What MLOps? Adpating the principles of DevOps to MLOps

Data and hyperparameters versioning, Description = The output of the model can change if the algorithm code or hyperparameters or data change. Code and hyperparameters can be controlled by developers, but not data -> harder than traditional software dev.. Iterative development and experimentations, Description = Model development depends on experimentations, with parameters tuning, sampling analysis and code corrections. The advantage of using versioning and MLOps is to track the complete lineage of the changes.. Testing, Description = Data quality tests, anomalies and data distribuition checks. Unitary tests on the preprocessing step. Track model metrics based on business problem as well as ensure algorithm fairness.. Security, Description = The model is consumed by applications that should have permission for only known users.. Production Monitoring, Description = Models in production requires continuous monitoring to make sure models are performing per expectation as they process new data. Infrastructure requirement, Description = During experimentations and production scaling may be necessary dynamically, e.g. increase of data volume.

What MLOps? Adpating the principles of DevOps to MLOps

- In traditional software engineering (software 1.0), developers write logic and rules (as code) that are well defined in the program space.
- In machine learning (software 2.0), data scientists write code to create a model that t
grounding.citation: What MLOps? Adpating the principles of DevOps to MLOps > How MLOps? MLOps framework > How MLOps?

concepts.title: MLOps Overview
concepts.summary: MLOps ensures that machine learning initiatives can keep pace with organizational growth by balancing business needs with technical complexity.

grounding.text:
How MLOps?

- As the organisation starts to grow, all machine learning initiatives should keep the pace.
- Business problems, balance on profit-costs will dictate the complexity of the human structure behind the MLOps.
- It is quiet different having a group of Data Scientists focused in different areas such as data ingestion, or worried about the scalability of the model to production.
- A Data Engineer focused on data ingestion will put the organization one step far from "garbage in, garbage out".
- A ML Engineers, DevOps focused on the deployment will leave the Data Scientists focused on "squeeze the best juice out of the model".
1. Robust automation and trust between teams.
2. The idea of collaboration and increased communication between teams.

How MLOps?

Subject Matter Expert, Tasks = Provide business questions, goals, or KPIs around which ML models should be framed. Continually evaluate and ensure that model performance aligns with or resolves the initial need.. Subject Matter Expert, MLOps requirements = Easy way to understand deployed model performance in business terms. Mechanism or feedback loop for model results that don't align with business expectations.. Data Scientist, Tasks = Build models that address the business question or needs brought by subject matter experts. Deliver operationalizable models so that they can be properly used in the production environment and with production data. Assess model quality (of both original and tests) in tandem with subject
grounding.citation: How MLOps?

concepts.title: MLOps Roles and Responsibilities
concepts.summary: Different roles (Software Engineers, DevOps, Model Risk Managers/Auditors, Machine Learning Architects, AI Engineers) have specific tasks and requirements within the MLOps lifecycle.

grounding.text:
How MLOps?

Software engineers, Tasks = Integrate ML models in the company's applications and systems. Ensure that ML models work seamlessly with other non-machine- learning-based applications.. Software engineers, MLOps requirements = Versioning and automatic tests. The ability to work in parallel on the same application.. DevOps, Tasks = Conduct and build operational systems and test for security, performance, availability. Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipeline management.. DevOps, MLOps requirements = Seamless integration of MLOps into the larger DevOps strategy of the enterprise. Seamless deployment pipeline.. Model risk managers/ auditors, Tasks = Minimize overall risk to the company as a result of ML models in production. Ensure compliance with internal and external requirements be.. Model risk managers/ auditors, MLOps requirements = Robust, likely automated, reporting tools on all models (currently or ever in production), including data lineage.. Machine learning architects, Tasks = Ensure a scalable and flexible environment for ML model pipelines, from design to development and monitoring. Introduce new technologies when appropriate that improve ML model performance in production.. Machine learning architects, MLOps requirements = High-level overview of models and their resources consumed. Ability to drill down into data pipelines to assess and adjust infrastructure needs. AI Engineer, Tasks = Design and optimize prompts for LLMs. Build RAG pipe
grounding.citation: How MLOps? > 05. Iteration and Life Cycle > 03. Model Deployment Requirements > 01. Model Development > 02. Production and Deployment > A machine learning model

01: Model Development

Unit data tests

01: Model Development

- Integrates PySpark.
- Allows SQL via the Spark SQL module.
- Open source.
- Version control.
- Persists the calculated metrics in a file system (locally or systems/cloud).
- Anomaly detectuin based on absolute or relative rates of changes and Z-Scores.

Unit data tests

- GE focuses on validation with basic statistics.
- Rich integrations(not developed as SQL first) but with hard learning curve.
- Open source.
- Version control.
- Own custom expectations, both at the column and individual record level.
- GE integrates out of the box with Slack so you can get notified when an expectation fails.
- Comes with a static website, the so called data docs , to visualize the results of a validation job;

concepts.title: Feature Definition Components
concepts.summary: Features are defined by several components including their source, name, type, and descriptive metadata.

grounding.text:
01: Model Development Feature Management

- o Source -Where the data comes from (table or method of computation).
- o Name -The name of the feature on the data source (e.g. table column), with a well-defined nomenclature.
- o Type -Int, Float, String allows for enforcing the correct behavior in filters and transformations.
- o Description  and  comments  added  by  the  Data  Scientists  to  keep  track  of  insights  about  the  feature,  meaning  of certain values (or where to find it). The impacts on the models, importance and metric summaries.
- o Some statistical measures about the features (cardinality, range).

concepts.title: Model Training Principles
concepts.summary: Training data must be accurately representative of reality, and model performance must align with specific business goals.

grounding.text:
01: Model Development Model training

- Training data is usually labeled for the prediction case with examples of what is being modeled.
- Should be na accurate projection of the reality. Sampling it in the proper way can be crucial to obtain the best results capable of generalizing the model.
- Performance metrics and business goals should be fully married on this honeymoon. The model should be for meeting the business goals.
- In a churn example, if a bank contacts all clients that the model predicts as possible cases of churn, that it should be exist a sweet point between the "saved" clients and the costs of the call center.
- There are a variety of models that work in various ways and have different pros and cons.
- It is important to note that some algorithms are more suited to certain tasks than others, but their selection also depends on what needs to be prioritized: performance, stability, interpretability, computation cost,
- The algorithm contains the basic formula, the parameters it learns are the operations and operands that make up this formula for that particular prediction task, and the hyperparameters are the ways that the algorithm may go to find these parameters. For example, in a decision tree (where data continues to be split in two according to what looks to be the best predictor in the subset that reached this path), one hyperparameter is the depth of the tree (i.e., the number of splits).
- When using labeled data, an evaluation dataset that is differen
grounding.citation: 01: Model Development Model training > 01: Model Development Model experimentation > Organization > Integration with Popular Tools > Reproducibility and Auditin

concepts.title: Problems with Non-Modular Deployment
concepts.summary: Non-modular deployment practices lead to issues such as inability to generalize across environments, lack of change tracking, difficulty in testing/debugging, lack of automation, and inability to audit processes.

grounding.text:
02: Model production and deployment

Nuno Rosa nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt Last week recap

02: Model Deployment and Production

Preamble

02: Model Deployment and Production

Hard-coded paths, Description = Cannot generalize across environments. No version control for data/model, Description = Can't track changes. No separation of concerns, Description = Hard to test, debug, or scale. Manual execution, Description = Not automatable. No logging, Description = Cannot audit

02: Model Deployment and Production

Modular design, Benefit = Easy to test and reuse. Central config/paths, Benefit = Flexible deployment. Separate concerns, Benefit = Scalable architecture. Parameterization possible, Benefit = Supports experimentation. Can integrate with CI/CD, Benefit = Automatable pipelines. Can add logging, Benefit = Monitoring& traceability. Can version data/model, Benefit = Auditability

concepts.title: Git Commands
concepts.summary: Git commands manage the workflow of version control, including staging, committing, pushing, pulling, switching branches, and merging histories.

grounding.text:
Git commands (there many other advanced)

1. add: The primary purpose of git add is to stage changes in your working directory for the next commit.
2. commit: Used to sabe the changes in your code after staging the changes with git add .
3. pull: Used to fetch and download content from a remote repository and immediately update the local repository to match that content.

Git commands (there many other advanced)

4. push: Used to upload your local repository's changes (commits) to a remote repository.

Git commands (there many other advanced)

5.checkout:  Refers to the act of switching to a specific branch or commit.

Git commands (there many other advanced)

6.merge: Refers to the process of combining two separate development histories into one. Git attempts to automatically merge the changes, but if there are conflicts, user intervention is needed.

concepts.title: Model Versioning and Foundation
concepts.summary: All project assets, including code, configurations, data pipelines, and models, must be version-controlled in a central Git repository, maintaining a structured directory for tracking experiments and schemas.

grounding.text:
02: Model Deployment and Production

1. Develop & Version (The Foundation)
- All project assets -code, configurations, data pipelines, and models -are version-controlled in a central Git repository.
- Maintains a structured directory (e.g., src/, data/, models/, .github/workflows/) to track experiments, schemas, and configurations.
2. Automate with GitHub Actions (The CI/CD Pipeline)
- Automated workflows are defined by a central configuration file (e.g., mlops.yml).
- Pipelines are triggered automatically by events like a code push, a Pull Request, or on a set schedule.
- Executes a sequential, automated process: Environment Setup ➔ Quality Checks & Linting ➔ Unit/Integration Testing ➔ Data Validation ➔ Model Training ➔ Evaluation ➔ Packaging ➔ Pushing Artifacts ➔ Notifications.
3. Artifacts & Registry (Asset Management)
- Outputs are pushed to secure cloud storage (e.g., S3, GCS).
- Utilizes tracking servers (like MLflow) to log experiments and a versioned Model Registry to manage approved models.
4. Deploy (Serving the Model)
- The approved model version is deployed to production using flexible architectural patterns: Batch processing, REST APIs, or Serverless functions.

02: Model Deployment and Production

This configuration file directly drives CI/CD tools (like GitHub Actions) to automatically:

02: Model Deployment and Production

- Trigger on code pushes or pull requests.
- Validate data and code integrity.
- Train, Evaluate, and Log model metrics.
- Package and Depl

concepts.title: Kedro Framework Overview
concepts.summary: Kedro is a development workflow framework designed to help create reproducible, maintainable, and modular production-ready code for data science projects.

grounding.text:
Pipelines orchestration using Kedro

- Kedro is a development workflow framework and a standard option for developing production-ready code. It was developed in QuantumBlack and McKinsey & Company and now hosted by the Linux Foundation (LF AI & Data).
- It was created to ensure reproducible, maintainable, and modular code, using the software engineering best practices on top of production ready data science code.
- Some successful applications of Kedro in real-world examples:
1. NASAas part of their cloud-based predictive engine that predicts impeded and unimpeded taxi out duration within the airspace.
2. JungleScout sped up the training and review of its sales estimation models 18 times with the help of Kedro.
3. Portuguese cyber security entity uses it as part of the process for detecting phishing campaigns.
4. One of the main portuguese banks on several use cases.
5. Tekmosel uses Kedro to run hundreds of feature engineering tasks and serve dozens of machine learning models in their production environment.
6. ElementAI improved their work efficiency by leveraging Kedro in their scheduling software to measure historical performance and create replay scenarios.

Pipelines orchestration using Kedro

https://docs.kedro.org/en/stable/get_started/install.html Normal version

1. NASAas part of their cloud-based predictive engine that predicts impeded and unimpeded taxi out duration within the airspace.
2. JungleScout sped up the training and review of its sales estimation models 18 times with the help of Kedro.
3. Portuguese cyber security entity uses it as part of the process for detecting phishing campaigns.
4. One of the main portuguese banks on several use cases.
5. Tekmosel uses Kedro to run hundreds of feature engineering tasks and serve dozens of machine learning models in their production environment.
6. ElementAI improved their work efficiency by leveraging Kedro in their scheduling software to measure historical performance and create replay scenarios.

Pipelines orchestration using Kedro

Pipelines orchestration using Kedro

concepts.title: Standard Project Structure
concepts.summary: A standard project structure organizes code and assets into specific folders like 'conf', '/data', '/docs', '/logs', '/notebooks', and '/src' to aid collaboration and maintenance.

grounding.text:
02: Model Deployment and Production

- Standard project structure that eases collaboration and code maintenance:
1. conf: Contains configuration files that specify vital details such as data sources (i.e. Data Catalog), model parameters, credentials, and logging information.
2. /data: Contains the input, intermediate and output data. It is organized into an eightlayer data engineering convention to clearly categorize and structure how data is processed. (TBD on the following slide)
3. /docs: Contains the files relating to the project documentation.
4. /logs: Contains the log files generated when pipeline runs are performed.
5. /notebooks: Contains Jupyter notebooks used in the project e.g., for experimentation or initial exploratory data analysis.
6. /src: Contains the source codes for the project, such as Python scripts for the pipeline steps, data processing, and model training.

02: Model Deployment and Production

No more mix of production code and experimentation notebooks. Hard-coded parameters in parameters.yml

Folders convention

Raw, Definition = Contains the sourced data model(s) that should never be changed, it forms your single source of truth to work from. These data models are typically un-typed in most cases e.g. csv, but this will vary from case to case.. Intermediate, Definition = Optional data model(s), which are introduced to type your raw data model(s), e.g. converting string based values into their current typed representation.. Primary, Definition = Dom
grounding.citation: 02: Model Deployment and Production > Folders convention > Other features

concepts.title: Data Layering Convention
concepts.summary: This convention defines specific data models (Raw, Intermediate, Primary, Feature, Model input, Models, Model output, Reporting) to structure data flow from source to final reporting.

concepts.title: MLOps Pipeline Tools
concepts.summary: Luigi is a pipeline framework with Task and Target building blocks, while ZenML is a framework that automatically tracks experiments using Python decorators and supports various ML/DL frameworks.

grounding.text:
02: Model Deployment and Production

- Developed by Spotify.
- Luigi pipeline has two building blocks, Task and Target.
- Taks is a transformation, and the target is an output.
- Integration of task with other ecosystem jobs such as Spark.
- Devoloped by Apache/AIrbnb
- Provides rich GUI with features including DAG visualization, execution progress monitoring, scheduling, and triggering.
- Adds Python decorators to your existing code, ZenML automatically tracks experiments.
- You can easily switch between local and cloud orchestration.
- ZenML works with any known framework, whether it's classical machine learning or deep learning. Also, it is agnostic when compared to Cloud providers.

Quality Assurance

- Unlikely to software engineering, quality assurance methodologies in MLOps is still giving the first steps, and it is too much dependent on the degree of maturity of the data organization and appended teams.
- The  entire  process  of  data  collection,  data  ingestion,  data  transformation,  feature  engineering  model  training  and  evaluation  metrics  should  all  be documented. It will be easier to validate every step of the process against organizational guidelines.
- Don't be spared about having assertions over your deploy pipeline. Create useful metrics as quality requirements for deployments.

Quality Assurance

Quality metrics, 1 = Model performance on production data (precision, accuracy, recall, e.g. na assertion on if the model accuracy falls bellow 90%). M
grounding.citation: 02: Model Deployment and Production > Quality Assurance > Ecommerce card fraud detection: > Healthcare: > Vehicle stock and sales recomendation:

concepts.title: Production Data Risk Mitigation
concepts.summary: This involves processes to handle unexpected changes in production data, such as features falling out of expected bounds, by triggering measures like sample rejection or warning messages.

grounding.text:
Risk

- mitigation · Production data changes for some reason (world war, seasonality, bug on a system). The value of a feature at inference time is out of the bounds.  Should  we  have  process  where  the  system  can  trigger  appropriate  measures,  such  as  rejecting  the  sample  or  dispatching  a warning message?
- In a classification problem, each event is scored and a threshold is defined for the final decision. Facing any seasonality event, one can have some dynamic threshold that  will  be  responsible  for  accepting  an  inference  output,  or  anomaly  detection  algorithm  to  find  the events that are outside of the application domain.
- Model  with  a  high  cardinality  of  feature  will  be  more  sensitive  to  these  issues:  the  number  of  combinations  is  exponential,  so  the performance could be unpredictable.
- Is the application domain of a new model already occupied by another model or heuristic rules? Should the outcome be combined? What strategy should be done to switch models, if it's the case?

Security

- There should be several access layers to the training dataset and model output. Models can leak data, for example, a model predicts how much someone is paid using the nearest neighbor algorithm. If one knows the zip code, age, and profession of a certain person registered on the service, the leak will happen...
- Connections to the external environment of the organization. If we need enrich the data while the model acting on real time, th
grounding.citation: Risk > Security

concepts.title: Model Sensitivity and Domain Issues
concepts.summary: Models with high feature cardinality are more sensitive to data issues because the number of possible combinations grows exponentially, leading to unpredictable performance.

concepts.title: MLOps Quality Assurance Process
concepts.summary: The entire MLOps process, from data collection to evaluation, must be documented to allow for validation against organizational guidelines.

grounding.text:
03: Model Requirements

Nuno Rosa nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt

Quality Assurance

- Unlikely to software engineering, quality assurance methodologies in MLOps is still giving the first steps, and it is too much dependent on the degree of maturity of the data organization and appended teams.
- The  entire  process  of  data  collection,  data  ingestion,  data  transformation,  feature  engineering  model  training  and  evaluation  metrics  should  all  be documented. It will be easier to validate every step of the process against organizational guidelines.
- Don't be spared about having assertions over your deploy pipeline. Create useful metrics as quality requirements for deployments.

Quality Assurance

Quality metrics, 1 = Model performance on production data (precision, accuracy, recall, e.g. na assertion on if the model accuracy falls bellow 90%). Metrics for computational performance ( average latency, e.g. an assertion if the 5% of score events take more than x milliseconds ).. Creative Scenarios, 1 = A feature (or more) that only has missing values. How does the perfomance changes? The distribuition of several features changes (one can force some events to have extreme values). Can the process be used for 3x times the quantity of the expected data.. Subpopulation analysis and model fairness, 1 = Overall metrics of the model are very good. But splitting the data into subpopulations it seems that there exists a concentration of false positives in a specific segment.

Qua
grounding.citation: 03: Model Requirements > Quality Assurance > 02: Model Deployment and Production > Ecommerce card fraud detection: > Healthcare: > Vehicle stock and sales recom

concepts.title: ML Security Risks
concepts.summary: ML models can leak sensitive data through predictions, such as in nearest neighbor algorithms, and external connections introduce risks like data tampering or perturbation attacks.

grounding.text:
Security

- There should be several access layers to the training dataset and model output. Models can leak data, for example, a model predicts how much someone is paid using the nearest neighbor algorithm. If one knows the zip code, age, and profession of a certain person registered on the service, the leak will happen...
- Connections to the external environment of the organization. If we need enrich the data while the model acting on real time, there is a chance an attacker be able to change the training data or to introduce small perturbations on the production data.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

- In this stage we need to have a ML artifact: a testable and deployable bundle of the project in the context of the CI/CD . This artifact can only be built after we have a modular code and data centralized in a repository with several elements to be bundled into an artifact:
1. Code for preprocessing, model, hyperparameters.
2. Environment with the libraries, specific versions, environment variables...
3. Training, validation and testing scenarios data.
- The artifact goes through a testing and standard process, and it is made available for deployment.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

It is crucial to have several validation steps to ensure the model fulfils all production requirements: from software to computation performance.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

concepts.title: Manual Model Implementation
concepts.summary: Manual implementation involves packaging models in a code repository, merging branches, and having engineers manually integrate the model into the application, leaving monitoring to the DevOps team.

grounding.text:
Manual Implementation

- Manually package the model (jupyter notebooks) in a structure that can be used and interfaced with other components and put it in a code repository.
- Merge a Git feature branch to the main branch.
- Software engineers must take the model from the code repository and manually integrate it into the application and the DevOps team is left with the task of monitoring the application in functional and performance terms.
- The weakest points of this solution are:
1. The setup is not resilient to changes in data and business needs.
2. Everything is handled manually , without any pipeline and automation technique.

Manual Implementation

become "person dependent"

Manual Implementation

, i.e. if the responsible for a part goes on vacations, the probability of risks and incapacity of solving issues increases.

Continuous Model Delivery

1.Feature Store -> data accessible to all teams and contains the data already cleaned and preprocessed.

Continuous Model Delivery

- 2.Automated Model Building and Analysis , which is an engineering of the model building phase. The output is a Modularized code, that allows you to standardize and automate the next steps.

Continuous Model Delivery

3.The Deploy Pipeline , which is a pipeline designed to put the model into production.

Continuous Model Delivery

4.The Automated Training pipeline, which is launched from the Deploy Pipeline or a Trigger and interfaces with the Feature Store to get the data that will be used for

concepts.title: Containerization Benefits
concepts.summary: Containers solve environmental compatibility issues by isolating and packaging an application and all its dependencies, making deployments reproducible and portable.

grounding.text:
Containerization

- Environmental compatibility is a headache, in addition to scaling, security, maintenance and upgrade of software and hardware.
- For hosted environments, we must work hard on the DevOps  to ensure the environments are the same. Usually, we do this part using  requirement file and locally installing python packages.
- Containers  are  tools  to  produce reproducible  environments  by  isolating  and  packaging  everything  that  your  program  needs ,  independent  of  the target/host environment where you intended to run the program.
- Many tasks can be isolated and served in such a way, including model deployments.
- Because of their architecture, they are  much lighter  on  resources  compared  to  a  full-blown virtual machines VM.
- Containers with our code/program/model in it can be  distributed  to  team  members  and  can  be publicly released with minimal environment incompatibility issues.

Containers

- A container is a standard unit of software that packages code and all its dependencies , so the application runs quickly and reliably from one computing environment to another.
- Using containers to separate different use cases makes our life easier to test-run new packages, modules and framework versions, without breaking the entire system. We do not need to install every tool on a local host.
- A container was an answer for some "heavy aspects" of the standard virtual machines. Virtual machines are an abstraction of physical hardware, turning on
grounding.citation: Containerization > Containers > 03: Model Deployment Requirements Docker > 1. Reproducibility: > 2. Portability: > 3. Integration: > 03: Model Deployment Requir

concepts.title: Containers vs Virtual Machines (VMs)
concepts.summary: VMs virtualize hardware, requiring a full OS copy for each instance, whereas containers virtualize the operating system, making them lighter and more efficient.

concepts.title: Docker and Containerization Basics
concepts.summary: A Dockerfile is essential for creating containers, which are isolated environments. An image is a snapshot of a Docker VM containing all necessary commands to build that image.

grounding.text:
03: Model Deployment Requirements

- The Dockerfile is at the heart of creating containers . Anytime you are creating a container, you must have the Dockerfile present in the current directory.
- An image is a snapshot of a Docker virtual machine at a certain time and place.
- Contains all the commands required to generate a specific image that is read by Docker to build that image automatically.
- Images are immutable . It's possible to erase a record after you've produced it, but you can't change it.
- Experimentations will not afect images in the new environment. Also it is easier to debug a problem.
- In the container the image offers all the required files to operate a set of processes.
- The applications are isolated from other processes that share the same kernel, so no one process can affect the work of others.

Dockerfile structure

# base image is an ubuntu FROM ubuntu:latest

Dockerfile structure

RUN apt-get install -y python3 -pip python3-dev buildessential

Dockerfile structure

RUN pip3 install -r requriments.txt

Dockerfile structure

CMD ["python3","main.py"]

Dockerfile structure

- Example of commands that can use in a dockerfile are:
- ❑ FROM - Creates a layer from the ubuntu
- ❑ RUN - Builds your container
- ❑ CMD - Specifies what command to run within the container

03: Model Deployment Requirements

- Pulls the official Python 3.10 'slim' image from Docker Hub -'slim' means it's a minimal Debian -based image w

concepts.title: .dockerignore usage
concepts.summary: The .dockerignore file specifies patterns to exclude files and directories from the Docker build context, preventing unnecessary or sensitive data from being sent to the daemon or included in the image.

grounding.text:
.dockerignore

- Before building accordingly to the instructions, it looks for a file named .dockerignore in the root directory of the context.
- If this file exists, it modifies the context to exclude files and directories that match patterns in it. This helps to avoid unnecessarily sending large or sensitive files and directories to the daemon and potentially adding them to images using ADD or COPY.
- Security issues to create this file: important files such as passwords, secret keys, .git folders contain a lot of information about the project, and you want to avoid to expose the details.
- When you write the Dockerfile, it's a general practice to use the COPY instruction to copy the files and folders inside the Docker build context. Each statement inside the Dockerfile results in building a new intermediate image layers -> cache invalidations and wastage of resources.

.dockerignore

Rule, 1 = Behaviour. # comment, 1 = Ignored.. */temp*, 1 = Exclude files and directories whose names start with temp in any immediate subdirectory of the root. For example, the plain file /somedir/temporary.txt is excluded, as is the directory /somedir/temp.. */*/temp*, 1 = Exclude files and directories starting with temp from any subdirectory that is two levels below the root. For example, /somedir/subdir/temporary.txt is excluded.. temp?, 1 = Exclude files and directories in the root directory whose names are a one-character extension of temp. For example, /tempa and /tempb are excluded.

03
grounding.citation: .dockerignore > 03: Model Deployment Requirements > Docker good practices

concepts.title: Docker Good Practices
concepts.summary: Good practices for Docker involve using a container per process, using separate containers for each service to allow horizontal scaling, and using unique tags for deployments instead of mutable tags like 'latest'.

grounding.text:
Docker good practices

```
DockerCompose is a tool that lets you define and run multi-container Docker applications with a simple
            version: "3.8"
            services:
              api:
                image: myregistry.com/ml-api:1.2.0
                ports:
                  - "5000:5000"
                deploy:
                  replicas: 4   #means running 4 instances of the same containerized service.

            redis:
              image: redis:6.2-alpine

            store:
              image: myregistry.com/feature-store:2.0.1

N:\\JAVA\IMS\information\

```

Docker good practices

- There are a series of good practices to implement each relevant component of Docker:
- Use a container for each process, which makes the debugging and orchestration far more easier. Also a container is meant to have the same lifetime as the app it contains.
- A separate container for each service allows you to grow one of your web servers horizontally to accommodate the extra traffic.
- There are tags that you can use for the basic image of the container. Avoid utilizing these tags for deployment containers since these tags will get changed often , and it might lead to discrepancies in the production environment, e.g. image: example.com/ml-api:latest
- Use unique tags for deployments . You can extend your production cluster to numerous nodes with ease. It prevents inconsistencies, and hosts will not fetch any other docker image version.

Docker good practices

concepts.title: Kubernetes Cluster Operation
concepts.summary: Kubernetes uses a master component to schedule containers onto nodes, and kubectl interacts with the Master via an API to manage deployments.

grounding.text:
Relevant concepts:

- kubectl tells the master to start application containers.
- Master schedules containers to run.

Kubernetes

- Once we have a cluster running, we can deploy containerized applications (one or many) using a certain configuration.
- This configuration is a set of instructions to Kubernetes to set up the application on the cluste r, where we map each container to the nodes.

Kubernetes

yml file to deploy

Kubernetes

- When creating a deployment, we need to specify container images as well as the number of copies of each image we want to instantiate .
- kubectl that uses an API to interact with the Master, will be responsible for the deployment.
- Replicas are clones that facilitate self-healing for pods. As with most processes and services, pods are liable to failure, errors, evictions, and deletion.

concepts.title: Containerization vs Virtual Machines
concepts.summary: Containers package an executable with its dependencies and share the host OS kernel, making them lightweight. Virtual machines use a hypervisor to emulate hardware, providing full isolation but being more resource-intensive.

grounding.text:
03:Model Deployment and Serving

Nuno Rosa nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt

Containerization (Recap)

- We are not going to serve from our local computers
- We need to deploy the model in other platforms to serve
- The best solution in terms of flexibility and isolations is a container.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

Containerization (Recap)

03: Model Deployment Requirements

- Virtual machines access the hardware of a physical machine through a hypervisor . The hypervisor creates an abstraction layer allowing the VM to access CPU, memory, and storage.
- Containers represent a package that includes an executable with the dependencies it needs to run . Each container shares the physical machine's hardware and operating system kernel with other containers.
- Virtual machines are typically more resource-intensive than containers and less lightweight and portable . Containers are a good choice for applications that need to be deployed quickly and optimized.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

Containerization (Recap)

03: Model Deployment Requirements

- Containers are good for Web services, microservices (local architecture), cloud computing and to Continuous Integration and Delivery.
- Virtual machines are good to create full isolated environments such as to testing a new software in a safely sandboxed environment, to the development of models, test their software in different operating systems. They are also good option for public clouds and hybrid solutions.

Docker good prac
grounding.citation: 03:Model Deployment and Serving > Containerization (Recap) > 03: Model Deployment Requirements > Docker good practices

concepts.title: Use Cases for Containers and VMs
concepts.summary: Containers are ideal for web services, microservices, and CI/CD due to their portability and speed. VMs are better suited for creating fully isolated environments, such as for testing in different OSs or in public/hybrid clouds.

- Virtual machines are good to create full isolated environments such as to testing a new software in a safely sandboxed environment, to the development of models, test their software in different operating systems. They are also good option for public clouds and hybrid solutions.

concepts.title: Docker Best Practices
concepts.summary: Good practices include using official/trusted images, utilizing .dockerignore, leveraging Docker layer caching, and running one container per process.

grounding.text:
03:Model Deployment and Serving

Nuno Rosa nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt

Containerization (Recap)

- We are not going to serve from our local computers
- We need to deploy the model in other platforms to serve
- The best solution in terms of flexibility and isolations is a container.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

Containerization (Recap)

03: Model Deployment Requirements

- Virtual machines access the hardware of a physical machine through a hypervisor . The hypervisor creates an abstraction layer allowing the VM to access CPU, memory, and storage.
- Containers represent a package that includes an executable with the dependencies it needs to run . Each container shares the physical machine's hardware and operating system kernel with other containers.
- Virtual machines are typically more resource-intensive than containers and less lightweight and portable . Containers are a good choice for applications that need to be deployed quickly and optimized.

concepts.title: Docker Good Practices
concepts.summary: Good practices for Docker involve using one container per process, giving each service its own container, and using unique, specific tags instead of mutable tags like 'latest' for deployments.

grounding.text:
Docker good practices

```
DockerCompose is a tool that lets you define and run multi-container Docker applications with a simple
            version: "3.8"
            services:
              api:
                image: myregistry.com/ml-api:1.2.0
                ports:
                  - "5000:5000"
                deploy:
                  replicas: 4   #means running 4 instances of the same containerized service.

            redis:
              image: redis:6.2-alpine

            store:
              image: myregistry.com/feature-store:2.0.1

N:\\JAVA\IMS\information\

```

Docker good practices

- There are a series of good practices to implement each relevant component of Docker:
- Use a container for each process, which makes the debugging and orchestration far more easier. Also a container is meant to have the same lifetime as the app it contains.
- A separate container for each service allows you to grow one of your web servers horizontally to accommodate the extra traffic.
- There are tags that you can use for the basic image of the container. Avoid utilizing these tags for deployment containers since these tags will get changed often , and it might lead to discrepancies in the production environment, e.g. image: example.com/ml-api:latest
- Use unique tags for deployments . You can extend your production cluster to numerous nodes with ease. It prevents inconsistencies, and hosts will not fetch any other docker image version.

Docker good practices

One can have

concepts.title: Kubernetes Pods
concepts.summary: A pod is the most basic unit in Kubernetes, representing a collection of containers that share structures like storage and networking.

grounding.text:
03: Model Deployment Requirements

·

03: Model Deployment Requirements

·

03: Model Deployment Requirements

·

03: Model Deployment Requirements

When an app is deployed as a container, it is encapsulated in a pod on a specific node. Pods are the most basic unit in Kubernetes . It is pods that are created and destroyed, not individual containers. One node can have several pods.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

A pod is a collection of containers that share structures and can be related between each other:

03: Model Deployment Requirements

- ❑ Storage (volumes)
- ❑ Networking and IP address

03: Model Deployment Requirements

These related containers can be a web server and a database for instance.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

We can also have a single container in a pod.

03: Model Deployment Requirements

Kubernetes

03: Model Deployment Requirements

To deploy your application, you need to expose the application to the outside world!

03: Model Deployment Requirements

- A Kubernetes Service is an abstraction layer which defines a logical set of Pods and enables external traffic exposure, load balancing and service discovery for those pods.
- A Service is defined using YAML or JSON and lets pods get deleted and replicated in the cluster with no influence on our app. Couplings between pods.
- The pods with IPs cannot be typically accessed from the outside. Instead, a service can be used to allow external connections.

yml file for service

Service Type, 1 = Us

concepts.title: Advantages of Distributed Systems
concepts.summary: Distributed systems offer advantages such as switching from single-machine setups to clusters, allowing different pipeline stages to request specific resources, and enabling data preparation or model training to utilize multiple machines or specialized hardware like GPUs/TPUs.

grounding.text:
Main advantages:

- ❑ Switch from a single machine to a distributed cluster.
- ❑ Different stages of your machine learning pipeline can request different amounts or types of resources.
- Data preparation step may benefit more from running on multiple machines.
- Model training may benefit more from computing on top of GPUs or tensor processing units (TPUs).

Why scalability is so relevant?

Business wants to grow and pay less... DevOps, MLOps want a stable platform that can run applications at scale...

Why scalability is so relevant?

One huge node in a Kubernetes cluster is not a good idea since all deployments will be affected in case a major incident. Moreover several nodes in a stand-by mode is not cost efficient.

Horizontal auto scaling in a cloud system:

- We have a node with a couple of running pods, and a service starts getting a lot of requests.
- It will scale up for more pods and and the load balancer of Kubernetes will redirect the requests for healthy pods (or our replicas).
- Those new pods will require more resources, so that will be placed on the same node and utilize dynamic RAM, or a new node will be added.
- You can also put some bounds on the resources allocated for the pods.
- If you know that a particular service should not consume more than X GiB, and there's a memory leak if it does, you instruct Kubernetes to kill the pod when RAM utilization reaches XGB. A new pod will start automatically.

Data Scientists and Kubernetes

- Wrapping Python machine
grounding.citation: Main advantages: > Why scalability is so relevant? > Horizontal auto scaling in a cloud system: > Data Scientists and Kubernetes > Kubeflow

concepts.title: Kubeflow Pipelines
concepts.summary: A Kubeflow Pipeline is structured as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) that defines a workflow, specifying required input parameters and wiring the output of one component as the input to the next stage.

grounding.text:
Kubeflow

GOAL: Kubeflow tell Kubernetes clusters about specificities and nuances that each execution of a machine learning library has, such as parallel training on multiple Kubernetes nodes.

Kubeflow Components and services

- Like Kedro, a Kubeflow Pipeline is a directed acyclic graph (DAG), with the respective components of the  workflow.
- Pipelines define input parameter required to run the pipeline and then show each component's output and how it is wired as input to the next stage in the graph (similar to Kedro).
- The upgrade here is to have an UI to define input parameters per run and then launch the job. We can save several outputs.

02: Model Deployment

Kubeflow Components and services

02: Model Deployment

These components work together to provide a scalable and secure system for running machine learning jobs (notebook-based jobs and also jobs outside of notebooks) .

Data Exploration with Notebooks

We can see it as a data exploration inspired by containers, with isolated environment per use case or service!

02: Model Deployment

02: Model Deployment

- Kubeflow Pipelines , we construct a DAG of operations in a configuration file (or via the UI) to schedule a series of orchestrated jobs on Kubeflow.
- Notebooks , we launch a notebook s

concepts.title: Resource Utilization in Model Serving
concepts.summary: Serving multiple models within a single container can increase resource utilization but introduces the risk of resource contention slowing down one model due to the other.

grounding.text:
03: Model Serving

- You want higher resource utilization . If the pricing model is idle, there are still 2 or more running containers to make sure the model is available and take up the requested number of resources. If a TorchServe container serves both the recommender and pricing models, you can use a higher percentage of resources. The tradeoff is you can have a situation where one model is slowed down because the other is competing for resources.
- L imit on the number of containers . Kubernetes has a per cluster limit, or the cluster is shared and there's a limit on the number of containers for the serving platform. It can enter in the budget management layer and resources.

03: Model Serving

Final picture Model Serving: The software that wraps the model to answer queries (e.g., FastAPI, MLflow). Next week.

03: Model Serving

Model Deployment: The infrastructure that hosts the served model, manages traffic, and ensures uptime (e.g., Kubernetes, Azure, Load Balancers, Docker).

concepts.title: Real-Time vs. Batch Inference

concepts.title: Shadow Testing
concepts.summary: Shadow testing is a mandatory practice where a challenger model runs alongside the champion model using the same input data without its predictions affecting the live system, ensuring safety and unbiased comparison.

concepts.title: Shadow Testing Disadvantages
concepts.summary: Shadow testing increases resource consumption (memory, processing power, storage) when multiple models run on the same data, leading to higher costs.

grounding.text:
Disadvantages:

- Multiple models are used for inferencing on the same data, this results in an increase in the consumption of resources such as memory, processing power, storage, etc, which indirectly results in an increase in cost. Hence, shadow testing is more expensive.

Shadow testing is not possible when:

1. The ground truth cannot be evaluated for both models. For an insurance fraud detection model, if to obtain the ground truth, we have to contact all clients, then we have to do it for the positive predictions of two models; it would increase the workload too much, because some frauds are only detected by only one model. As a result, randomly applying only the new. model to a small fraction of the requests will allow the workload to remain constant. *
2. The objective to optimize is only indirectly related to the performance of the prediction. Imagine an ad engine based on an ML model that predicts if a user will click on the ad. Now imagine that it is evaluated on the buy rate, i.e., whether the user bought the product or service.

Shadow testing is not possible when:

*Another example is a recommendation engine where the prediction gives a list of items on which a given customer is likely to click if there are presented.  So it will be impossible to know what would regarding to items that were not presented to the client.

03: Model Serving

A/B testing

03: Model Serving

- The total number of times a prediction needs to be made does not change, hence the resource
grounding.citation: Disadvantages: > Shadow testing is not possible when: > 03: Model Serving > Statistical protocol:

concepts.summary: A/B testing requires careful data splitting to avoid sampling bias, is often harder to implement than shadow testing, and carries risks if the tested model degrades.

concepts.title: ML Model Monitoring Levels
concepts.summary: ML models must be monitored at three distinct levels: resource, performance, and prediction explanation.

grounding.text:
MLOps Course overview

Nuno Rosa nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Machine learning models need to be monitored at three levels:
1. At the resource level, including ensuring the model is running correctly in the production environment.
2. At  the  performance  level,  meaning  monitoring  is  questioning  if  the  model  is  still  an  accurate  representation  of  the pattern of new incoming data or if it is performing as well as it did during the design phase.
3. Predictions must come with an explanation (i.e., which features have the most influence on the prediction). This kind of information is usually computed with techniques such as Shapley value computation and should be logged to identify potential issues with the model.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Model performance monitoring attempts to track this degradation, and, at an appropriate time, it will also trigger the retraining of the model.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- The real world is not perfect!!! Its realism has been challenging Machine Learning perfect assumptions: data tends to change over the time.
- Models trained in the past may become less accurate with the time evolution.
- Since covid, payments changed. Card present transaction are decreasing (we start to have long "online black fridays").
- Models to predict revenue of merchants had to adapt to this new profilling.
- Recommendation systems and marketing campaigns had to switch from an onpremise ecosystem to an o
grounding.citation: MLOps Course overview > 04: Monitoring and feedback loop

concepts.title: Model Performance and Data Drift
concepts.summary: Data drift does not imply a change in the relationship between input and output variables, but an input distribution shift can make the model less capable of predicting labels.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Corresponds to a change on the distribuition of the input features used to train the model.
- This could be introducing new values of categorical features, natural and external shifts in data (as the temperture variations across the year), changes on data quality.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Definition: Let be P(x) the marginal probability of the input features x, and P(y/x) the condition probability of the targets y given the input features of x.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

$$P _ { t r a i n } ( x ) & \neq P _ { t e s t } ( x ) \\ P _ { t r a i n } ( y / x ) & = P _ { t e s t } ( y / x )$$

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Data drift does not mean a change in the relationship between the input variables and the output.
- Input distribution shift makes the model less capable of predicting labels.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Data drift/Covariate shift

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Actions that one should take in order to detect data drift:
1. Monitor the input data : If we have a profilling system and unit data test on the input data (Great expectations and Pydeequ) we can easily detect any significant change on the data distribution. Data visualization of the feature distribuition can also help.
2. Monitor the performance of the model : any degradation of the model performance can be a consequence of the presence of some data drift.
3. Drift detection methods: statistical tests and specialized algorithms to de
grounding.citation: 04: Monitoring and feedback loop > Statistical tests

$$P _ { t r a i n } ( x ) & \neq P _ { t e s t } ( x ) \\ P _ { t r a i n } ( y / x ) & = P _ { t e s t } ( y / x )$$

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Data drift does not mean a change in the relationship between the input variables and the output.
- Input distribution shift makes the model less capable of predicting labels.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Data drift/Covariate shift

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Actions that one should take in order to detect data drift:
1. Monitor the input data : If we have a profilling system and unit data test on the input data (Great expectations and Pydeequ) we can easily detect any significant change on the data distribution. Data visualization of the feature distribuition can also help.
2. Monitor the performance of the model : any degradation of the model performance can be a consequence of the presence of some data drift.

concepts.title: PSI Considerations
concepts.summary: When using PSI, it is crucial not to switch the order of reference and production data, and binning continuous variables requires exploration to find an optimal number of bins.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

PSI value, 1 = Conclusion. < 0.1, 1 = No significant population change. <0.2, 1 = Moderate population change. >= 0.2, 1 = Significant population change

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Population Stability Index

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- PSI is not symmetric, do not switch the order of the reference and production data.
- Binning is important: when considering continuous variables, the number of bins affect PSI values. Some exploration is required, just to find the optimal number of bins.
- Don't be afraid of double-checking PSI values with a visual component of a plot with the respective distributions.
- PSI has a problem with 0 probability events (remember the formula log(actual/expected)).

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Final challenge: Can I use PSI for feature selection ? What would be the implications?

concepts.title: JS Divergence
concepts.summary: Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence is typically applied to each feature independently and measures how each feature has diverged from baseline values, rather than being a multivariate drift measurement.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- JS divergence is typically applied to each feature independently.
- It is not designed as a covariant feature measurement but rather a metric that shows how each feature has diverged independently from the baseline values.
- It's not a mulit -variate drift measurement
- The traditional receipt for this kind of tests is start by binning data:
1. Find a robust method (taking into consideration a certain scale of features and data.
2. Create mixture distribution for comparison using the production and baseline distributions. This makes JS divergence a symmetric metric (such as PSI). 0 bins are handled naturally (mixture terms of distributions in log).

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

$$\sum p ( x ) \, \log \left ( \frac { 2 \ p ( x ) } { q ( x ) + p ( x ) } \right ) \, + \, \sum q ( x ) \, \log \left ( \frac { 2 \ q ( x ) } { q ( x ) + p ( x ) } \right ) \, \rightarrow \, \sum q ( x ) \, \log \left ( 2 \right )$$

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

(0*ln(0) = 0) as the function is smooth and has a limit as it approaches 0

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

3. Compare production and baseline to mixture.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

KS(Kolmogorov-Smirnov) test

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- We can use KS test as a nonparametric statistical test, therefore does not make any assumptions about the underlying distribution.
- The null hypothesis is that the two samples come from the same distribution. The KS test is applied to reject or

concepts.title: Limitations of KS Test
concepts.summary: Limitations of the KS test include its applicability only to continuous distributions, its tendency to be more sensitive near the center than the tails, and the requirement that the underlying distribution must be fully specified.

concepts.title: Univariate Drift Detection Limitations
concepts.summary: Univariate drift detection treats each feature in isolation, which fails when the relationship between features changes, even if individual feature distributions remain standard.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

When does univariate drift detection fail?

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- The applied statistical tests are univariate! Each feature is treated in isolation from the rest.
- In the 10th week (blue), there is a strong positive correlation between the two features.
- In the 16th week (yellow), the marginal univariate distribution of each feature is still standard normal as before but are negatively correlated.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Strong covariate shift No change on the distribution of the features Need of a multivariate approach that can capture the changes in the joint features.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Multivariate approach

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

1. Dimensionality reduction by identifying the directions of our data that contain most of the variance.
2. Data reconstruction using the eigenvectors and the transformed data.
3. Estimate the expected reconstruction error: the result will not be the same as the original data due to the lossy compression process

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Multivariate approach

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Choose a reference period to learn the PCA mapping - week 9.
- Use the learned mapping to compress and decompress week 10 and week 16.
- Week 16 presents a high reconstruction error - covariate shift detection.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Multivariate approach issues

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Expected reconstruction error:

concepts.title: Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN)
concepts.summary: ADWIN is an algorithm that uses a sliding window approach to detect drift by comparing means between sub-windows of incoming data.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- The Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN) algorithm uses a sliding window approach to detect drift . Window size is fixed and ADWIN slides the fixed window for detecting any change on the newly arriving data.
- When two sub-windows show distinct means in the new observations the older sub-window is dropped.
- A user defined threshold is set to trigger a warning that drift is detected. If the absolute difference between the two means derived from two sub-windows exceeds the pre-defined threshold, an alarm is generated .

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Useful for signal analysis, time series...

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Concept Drift

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Concept drift is different from covariate shift and label drift : it is not related to the data distribution or the class distribution but instead is related to the relationship between both .
- It is mainly a result of changes in the real-world phenomena being modeled, nevertheless also can be explained by temporary changes that occur in the data that is being collected, or a bias sample in the training process.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Definition: Let be P(y/x) the condition probability of the target label y given the input features of x.

concepts.title: Concept Drift Detection Methods
concepts.summary: Drift can be detected using data distribution-based methods (using distance measures), performance-based methods, or contextual-based detectors.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- The Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN) algorithm uses a sliding window approach to detect drift . Window size is fixed and ADWIN slides the fixed window for detecting any change on the newly arriving data.
- When two sub-windows show distinct means in the new observations the older sub-window is dropped.
- A user defined threshold is set to trigger a warning that drift is detected. If the absolute difference between the two means derived from two sub-windows exceeds the pre-defined threshold, an alarm is generated .

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Useful for signal analysis, time series...

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Concept Drift

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Concept drift is different from covariate shift and label drift : it is not related to the data distribution or the class distribution but instead is related to the relationship between both .
- It is mainly a result of changes in the real-world phenomena being modeled, nevertheless also can be explained by temporary changes that occur in the data that is being collected, or a bias sample in the training process.

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

concepts.title: Concept Drift Handling Strategies
concepts.summary: There are several strategies to deal with concept drift, including passive updates, active detection, data weighting, ensemble methods, and resampling.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Performance-based detection

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Multiple hypothesis based detection

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Contextual-based detection

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Handling Concept Drift

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- We have some strategies to deal with concept drift:
1. A passive one; the model is updated continuously every time new data instances are received. This one is quite effective with gradual drifts and recurring concepts. We can include here online learning, where the many real-life applications run in streaming data, and use this methodology for each new sample at time.
2. An active strategy where the model only gets updated when a drift is detected. This strategy works well when the drift is abrupt.
3. Introduce weight to your data (actually is a simple variation of the previous strategy. You can give more weight to your recent data when updating the model). An alternative is to remove bias or systematic changes, such as seasonality effects. Feature dropping can play na important role.
4. An ensemble of models. Keep the old model, as static one with the patterns and combine it with a new one, with the recent patterns (boosting type inspiration).
5. A resampling : using instant selection to find a subsample where the model is weak, but the underlying distribution of the overall population is left intact. Improve the model against those.
