concepts.title: Model Error Types
concepts.summary: Hallucination refers to a factual or logic error in a model's output, while Data Drift describes a mismatch in the distribution or pattern of the input data.

grounding.text:
04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Primary Nature, Hallucination = A factual/logic error.. Primary Nature, Data Drift = A distribution/pattern mismatch.. Dependency on Time, Hallucination = Can happen instantly.. Dependency on Time, Data Drift = Happens over time (temporal).. Root Cause, Hallucination = Model architecture/training constraints.. Root Cause, Data Drift = Real-world changes (Covariate/Concept shift).

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

Final summary

04: Monitoring and feedback loop

- Detect feature changes between training and production . (In the lack of unit data tests it can be a way of detecting pipeline breaks or problems transforming data).
- Detect prediction distribution shifts between two production periods as a proxy for performance changes (especially useful in delayed ground truth scenarios).
- Use drift as a signal for when to retrain -and how often to retrain.
- Data discovery : new data to label or clusters of new data that are problematic for the model in unstructured data.

Take home lesson:

Sometimes non-practitioners tend to create an obsession of perfecting the mathematics of catching data changes. In practice, Real data changes all the time in production and many models extend well to this modified data.

Take home lesson:

The goal of using drift metrics is to have a solid, stable and strongly useful metric that enables troubleshooting.
grounding.citation: 04: Monitoring and feedback loop > Take home lesson:

concepts.title: Model Governance Definition
concepts.summary: Model governance is the set of controls placed on a business to ensure it meets its responsibilities to all stakeholders, covering financial, legal, and ethical obligations.

grounding.text:
05: Model Governance

Nuno Rosa nagostinho@novaims.unl.pt

05: Model Governance

- A machine learning model is not isolated from society rules and and law, and more important, the past, where the model was trained, cannot antecipate the evolving problems of the future.
- Governance is the set of controls placed on a business to ensure that it delivers on its responsibilities to all stakeholders, from shareholders and employees to the public and national governments. These responsibilities include financial, legal, and ethical obligations.

Components of Responsible AI, as an human-centered approach

Assurance that models are designed and behave in ways aligned with their purpose, Accountability = Central control, management, and the ability to audit the enterprise AI effort (no shadow IT!). Assurance that data used for AI projects comes from compliant and unbiased sources, Accountability = An overall view of which teams are using what data, how, and in which models. Explainability, meaning the result of AI systems should be explainable for everyone., Accountability = Trust that data is reliable and being collected in accordance with regulation as well as a centralized understanding of which model s are being used for which business process (traceability).

05: Model Governance

ML Governance is a new challenge and in the future will no be optional.

05: Model Governance

The integration of MLOps and model governance depends on two aspects:

05: Model Governance

1. Strength o

concepts.title: Tree Surrogates
concepts.summary: Tree Surrogates are interpretable models trained to approximate the predictions of a black-box model, allowing conclusions about the black-box model to be drawn by interpreting the surrogate.

grounding.text:
05: Model Governance

- ❑ Tree Surrogates are an interpretable model that is trained to approximate the predictions of a black-box model. We can draw conclusions about the black-box model by interpreting the surrogate model. The policy trees are easily human interpretable and provide quantitative predictions of future behaviour. Can be used both globally and locally.

05: Model Governance

- ❑ Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM) is an interpretable model developed at Microsoft Research. It is a tree-based, cyclic gradient boosting Generalized Additive Model with automatic interaction detection. EBMs are often as accurate as state-of-the-art blackbox models while remaining completely interpretable. Although EBMs are often slower to train than other modern algorithms, EBMs are extremely compact and fast at prediction time. EBM can be used both globally and locally.

Can be used both globally and locally.

05: Model Governance

- ❑ Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM) is an interpretable model developed at Microsoft Research. It is a tree-based, cyclic gradient boosting Generalized Additive Model with automatic interaction detection. EBMs are often as accurate as state-of-the-art blackbox models while remaining completely interpretable. Although EBMs are often slower to train than other modern algorithms, EBMs are extremely compact and fast at prediction time. EBM can be used both globally and locally.

05: Model Governance

https://christophm.github.io/interpretable-ml-book/

05: Model Governance

Permutation Importance

05: Model Governance

We measure the importance of a feature by calculating the increase in the model's prediction error after permuting the feature.

05: Model Governance

A feature is important if shuffling its values increases the model error (the model relied on the feature for the prediction)

05: Model Governance

https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.01489

05: Model Governance

A feature is 'unimportant' if shuffling its values leaves the model error unchanged, because in this case the model ignored t

concepts.title: Shapley Value Definition
concepts.summary: The Shapley value of a feature value represents its contribution to the prediction, calculated by averaging the change in prediction when the feature joins different coalitions of other features.

grounding.text:
much

The feature values enter a game in random order. All feature values participate, contributing to the prediction. The Shapley value of a feature value is the average change in the prediction that the coalition already in the game receives when the feature value joins them.

05: Model Governance

SHAP

05: Model Governance

- The Shapley value of a feature value is its contribution to the payout, weighted and summed over all possible feature value combinations:

05: Model Governance

where S is a subset of the features used in the model, x is the vector of feature values of the instance to be explained and p the number of features. val(S) is the prediction for feature values in set S that are marginalized over features that are not included in set S:

05: Model Governance

$$\ v a l _ { x } ( S ) = \int \hat { f } \left ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { p } \right ) d \mathbb { P } _ { x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } } - E _ { X } ( \hat { f } \left ( X \right ) ) \quad \quad \ v a l _ { x } ( S ) = \ v a l _ { x } ( \{ 1 , 3 \} ) = \int _ { \mathbb { R } } \hat { f } \left ( x _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , X _ { 4 } \right ) d \mathbb { P } _ { X _ { 2 } , X _ { 4 } } - E _ { X } ( \hat { f } \left ( X \right ) )$$

05: Model Governance

Evaluate the prediction for the coalition S consisting of feature values x1 and x3

05: Model Governance

- Summing all shapley values of all features, we obtain:

05: Model Governance

$$\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \phi _ { j } = \hat { f } ( x ) - E

concepts.title: Course Evaluation Methods
concepts.summary: The course grade is determined by two components: an individual examination and a group project.

grounding.text:
Evaluation method

- Individual examination : multiple choice questions ( 30% of the final grade ).
1. 1st Exam 02/07/2026 15:00:00
2. 2nd Exam 18/07/2026 09:00:00
- Group project with 3-5 students ( 70% of the final grade ):
1. The aim of the project is to simulate the real-world process of deploying machine learning models . More specifically, you need to build a standard and efficient Machine Learning pipeline.
2. The deliverables of the project should be:
- a) Project Report : In at most 6 pages. You should present the definition of the problem, objective, metrics, used technology, risk analysis and conclusions. 10-15 minutes presentation (+ 5-10 minutes of discussion) in the end of the trimester.
- b) Code and data : Code associated with the project, a small sample of the data/inputs/outputs if needed, and all steps necessary to replicate your project should be provided along with/in the report. A link to your github is acceptable here (provide it at the fro nt page of the report). The instructions to run it should also be included.
3. Dealines:
- a. Until the end of the week 08/05 group constitutions.
- b. Week of the 13/05 Midterm PdS with each group : dataset should be already choosen.
- c. Final date 30/06.
grounding.citation: Evaluation method

concepts.summary: MLFlow is fundamental for processes like creating versioned models.

grounding.text:
Machine Learning Operations Spring 2025

Nuno Rosa 1st Exam 27/06/2025  Version A

Name of the student:                                                                                                                  Number: Duration of the exam: 60 minutes In each question there exists information of how much each one is worth on a scale of 0 to 20 pts . A multiple- choice wrong answer corresponds to a certain penalty. In each question you will see (score/ -penalty). Write the capital letter correspondent to the right answer.

- 1- For which process is the MLFlow fundamental? (1 pts/ -0.15pts)

- A) Creating versioned models.

- B) Creating virtual enviroments.

- C) Train models.

- D) Apply data unit tests.

Choose the right option: ___   A

- 2- While having a model in production, we keep the development of new models, and we can put the new ones as challengers for the champion model. What are the advantages of shadow testing? (1 pts/ -0.15pts)

- A) We will test multiple models in different subpopulations, so that we need a statistical protocol.

- B) With shadow testing we save on the consumption of resources.

- C) We test against the same input, and it is safer since there is no room for sampling bias on the target events.

Choose the right option: ___  C

- 3The Machine Learning models usually have a trade-off between interpretability and performance. Which option is the most correct to describe the following picture? (1 pts/ -0.15pts)
- A) A- Neural Networks, B- Lin

concepts.title: Git Commands
concepts.summary: Git commands are used to manage changes in a local repository and synchronize them with a remote repository.

grounding.text:
- 10- Which command in Git is used to upload your local repository's changes (commits) to a remote repository? (1 pts/ -0.15pts)

- A) Push.

- B) Pull.

- C) Merge.

Choose the right option: ___ A

- 11- In what order are the following phases executed in a machine learning project?(1 pts/ -0.15pts)

I - Selection of ML algorithm II - Data Exploration III - Definition of the business use case IV - Model monitoring V - Model operationalization VI - Model Development

- A) I, II, III, IV, V, VI

- B) III, II, I, VI, V, IV

- C) II, III, I, VI, IV, V

- D) II, I, III, VI, IV, V

Choose the right option: ___ B

- 12- What is the main difference between covarite shift and label drift? (1 pts/ -0.15pts)

- A) Covariate shift is a consequence of label drift.

- B) Covariate shift is related to a change in the distribution of the input variables of the model. The label drift corresponds to a change in the distribution of the target variable of the model.

- C) Label drift is related to a change in the distribution of the input variables of the model. The covariate shift corresponds to a change in the distribution of the target variable of the model.

Choose the right option: ___  B

- 13- What are the costs of adopting a passive strategy to handle concept drift (1 pts/ -0.15pts)?

- A) Costs of keeping the label at a high pace.

- B) Costs of feedback systems and data drift solid tests.

- C) Takes time to explore and find the optimal solution.

Choose the right option: ___  A

14Dur

- 10- Which command in Git is used to upload your local repository's changes (commits) to a remote repository? (1 pts/ -0.15pts)

- A) Push.

- B) Pull.

- C) Merge.

Choose the right option: ___ A

- 11- In what order are the following phases executed in a machine learning project?(1 pts/ -0.15pts)

I - Selection of ML algorithm II - Data Exploration III - Definition of the business use case IV - Model monitoring V - Model operationalization VI - Model Development

- A) I, II, III, IV, V, VI

- B) III, II, I, VI, V, IV

- C) II, III, I, VI, IV, V

- D) II, I, III, VI, IV, V

Choose the right option: ___ B

- 12- What is the main difference between covarite shift and label drift? (1 pts/ -0.15pts)

- A) Covariate shift is a consequence of label drift.

- B) Covariate shift is related to a change in the distribution of the input variables of the model. The label drift corresponds to a change in the distribution of the target variable of the model.

- C) Label drift is related to a change in the distribution of the input variables of the model. The covariate shift corresponds to a change in the distribution of the target variable of the model.

Choose the right option: ___  B

- 13- What are the costs of adopting a passive strategy to handle concept drift (1 pts/ -0.15pts)?

- A) Costs of keeping the label at a high pace.

- B) Costs of feedback systems and data drift solid tests.

- C) Takes time to explore and find the optimal solution.

Choose the right option: ___  A

concepts.summary: This week focuses on the foundational principles of DevOps and how they apply to the Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) framework, including suitable roles for different parts of the framework.

grounding.text:
# MLOps Agenda


##### Week 1

Focus on the principles from DevOps to MLOps. Whow DevOps helped Machine Learning? Suitable roles for each part of the MLOps framework (where should be the Data Engineer, Data Scientist, etc). Data unit tests and feature store concepts (why are they relevant).


##### Week 2

Model experimentation concepts (such as versioning , registry).


##### Week 3

Focus the study on Continues Integration (those git branches differences). No questions about kedro, only about concepts of modular code and pipelines orchestration (such as its relevance)


##### Week 4

Focus on Manual Implementation vs Continuous Model Delivery vs Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery of pipeline

Relevance of preproduction environments. Concepts of Containers, Virtual Environments and Kubernetes basics. The difference between them. How works dockerfile.


##### Week 5

Focus on model serving part such as Shadow testing, A/B testing. No kubeflow.


##### Week 6

I will not ask about methods outside the ones discussed in the class: PSI, Jensen, KS, Kulback, multivariate analysis using PCA. Focus on the difference between the different types of drift and try to connect with some real world life examples. How we handle concept drift. After detecting model degradation, what actions should be taken to mitigate the problem?


##### Week 7

Intersection of MLOps with Governance and Interpretability/Explainability.  Then just check Shap vs Permutation importance (no math, just

questions.stem: Which of the following is a basis expectation related to data integrity?

options.text: Analyzing account activity patterns
options.rationale: Analyzing account activity patterns is an example of a possible business expectation, not a listed basis expectation type.

options.text: Null Values
options.rationale: Null Values is a basis expectation.

options.text: Feature store creation
options.rationale: Feature store creation is a component of ML infrastructure, not a listed basis expectation type.

options.text: Model training
options.rationale: Model training is a phase of model development, not a listed basis expectation type.

questions.explanation: Null Values is one of the basis expectations when defining operational rules for ML.
hints: Review the section detailing 'Basis Expectations'.

questions.stem: What is the primary function of a feature store in relation to business entities?

options.text: To handle null values in raw data before feature creation.
options.rationale: Handling null values is a general data expectation, but the feature store's primary role is feature centralization.

options.text: To serve as a central repository for features associated with business entities.
options.rationale: Feature stores are repositories of different features associated with business entities that are created and stored in a central location for easier reuse.

options.text: To manage the deployment pipeline for machine learning models.
options.rationale: Feature stores focus on managing features, not the overall model deployment pipeline.

options.text: To exclusively store data for real-time inference needs.
options.rationale: Feature stores combine both offline storage for batch processing and online storage for real-time needs.

questions.explanation: Feature stores act as central repositories where features linked to specific business entities are created and stored to facilitate reuse across the organization.
hints: Consider what a 'repository' means in the context of data management.
The core purpose relates to organizing features tied to business entities.

questions.stem: Which of the following scenarios represents a business expectation that needs to be validated during data assessment?

options.text: The model training phase must complete within a specific time limit.
options.rationale: Time limits are operational constraints, not direct business expectations related to data content.

options.text: If the send_id is null, the operation type must always be deposit-cash.
options.rationale: This is a possible business expectation regarding data integrity.

options.text: The feature store must have both an offline and an online component.
options.rationale: This describes a technical characteristic of feature stores, not a business rule for data validation.

options.text: The system must handle all data ingestion without any null values.
options.rationale: The text mentions checking for null values, but does not mandate that all values must be present.

questions.explanation: Business expectations are specific rules derived from the business context that data must adhere to, such as ensuring that if a send_id is null, the operation type is deposit-cash.
hints: Review the section detailing 'Possible business expectations'.
Focus on rules that define correct data states based on business logic.

questions.stem: Which component of a feature store is typically used for low-latency, real-time predictions?

options.text: The Offline Store
options.rationale: The Offline Store is used for historical batch training, not real-time predictions.

options.text: The Gold Layer
options.rationale: The Gold layer is where the feature store integrates, but it is not the low-latency store itself.

options.text: The Online Store
options.rationale: The Online Store is designed for low-latency, real-time predictions.

options.text: The Raw Data Layer
options.rationale: The Raw Data Layer is the source, not the component used for low-latency serving.

questions.explanation: The Online Store component of a feature store is specifically designed to provide features quickly for real-time inference.
hints: Consider the word 'real-time' and what kind of database supports fast lookups.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of treating features as first-class citizens in the context of ML?

options.text: To simplify the data warehousing process for general business reporting.
options.rationale: Feature stores are specifically designed for machine learning features, not general business reporting.

options.text: To ensure features are stored centrally with metadata, lineage, and freshness information.
options.rationale: Treating features as first-class citizens means storing them centrally with necessary metadata to prevent skew.

options.text: To exclusively support offline, slower computation for model training.
options.rationale: Feature stores typically combine both an offline part and an online part for different needs.

options.text: To eliminate the need for any feature engineering from raw data.
options.rationale: While feature stores help manage features, they do not eliminate the process of creating them from raw data.

questions.explanation: Treating features as first-class citizens means managing them centrally with metadata, lineage, and freshness information, which is crucial for preventing training-serving skew.
hints: Consider what information is tracked when a feature is treated as a core asset.
The goal relates to consistency between training and serving environments.

questions.stem: Which component of a feature definition specifies where the data originates, such as a table or a computational method?

options.text: Name
options.rationale: The Name component identifies the feature's name on the data source, not its origin.

options.text: Type
options.rationale: The Type component defines the data type, like Int or Float, for behavior enforcement.

options.text: Source
options.rationale: The Source component specifies where the data comes from, which can be a table or a method of computation.

options.text: Description
options.rationale: The Description component provides insights, meaning of values, and model impacts, not the data origin.

questions.explanation: The Source component is indicating where the data originates, whether it is from a table or a method of computation.
hints: Consider what question 'where' the component answers.
Look for the term related to data origin.

questions.stem: When a feature is generated, what two core components must be defined as part of the universal process?

options.text: The feature's name and its statistical measures.
options.rationale: While the name and statistical measures are part of feature management, the universal process requires defining the feature's type and its calculation recipe.

options.text: The data source and the model's importance score.
options.rationale: The data source is part of feature management, but the universal process focuses on the feature's type and calculation recipe.

options.text: Its type (natural or aggregated) and its calculation recipe.
options.rationale: The universal process mandates defining the feature's type (natural or aggregated) and specifying a calculation recipe.

options.text: The description and the cardinality of the feature.
options.rationale: Description and cardinality are metadata associated with the feature, not the two defining elements of the generation process itself.

questions.explanation: The universal process for feature generation requires defining two things: whether the feature is natural or aggregated (its type), and the specific steps needed to compute it (its calculation recipe).
hints: Focus on the core requirements of the 'universal process' mentioned.
Consider what defines *how* a feature is created, not just what it is.

questions.stem: What is the initial step in the Feature Execution Pipeline?

options.text: Standardized Compute
options.rationale: Standardized Compute occurs after checking the cache if the feature is not found.

options.text: Persistence
options.rationale: Persistence happens after the feature vector is generated.

options.text: Ingestion
options.rationale: The pipeline begins when the system receives an event, which is the ingestion step.

options.text: Smart Caching
options.rationale: Smart Caching is the second step, which checks the Feature Store cache.

questions.explanation: The Feature Execution Pipeline starts with Ingestion, where the system receives an event and queries the Feature Dictionary.
hints: Think about the very first action the pipeline takes when an event arrives.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a strategy for creating new features?

options.text: Data cleaning
options.rationale: Data cleaning is a data preparation step, not explicitly listed as a feature creation strategy.

options.text: Feature synthesis
options.rationale: Feature synthesis is one of the strategies for creating new features.

options.text: Model training
options.rationale: Model training is the process of using features, not the strategy for creating them.

options.text: Hyperparameter tuning
options.rationale: Hyperparameter tuning optimizes model settings, which is separate from feature creation strategies.

questions.stem: When implementing Quality Assurance in MLOps, which of the following is a necessary component for validating the entire process?

options.text: Documenting the entire lifecycle from data collection through evaluation metrics.

questions.stem: What is a primary concern addressed by production data risk mitigation regarding feature values during inference?

options.text: Ensuring the model's training data is completely anonymized.
options.rationale: Data anonymization is a security concern, not the direct focus of handling out-of-bounds feature values.

options.text: Handling situations where a feature's value falls outside its expected range.
options.rationale: Production data risk mitigation addresses when a feature's value at inference time is out of the expected bounds.

options.text: Preventing the model from making any predictions if external data enrichment is required.
options.rationale: The text mentions external connections but focuses on handling data changes, not preventing all predictions.

options.text: Managing the complexity introduced by having multiple models operating in the same application domain.
options.rationale: While model coexistence is mentioned, the core risk mitigation process described relates to data integrity during inference.

questions.explanation: Production data risk mitigation is designed to handle unexpected changes in production data, specifically when a feature's value at inference time falls outside its expected bounds.

questions.stem: What is a primary consequence of a model having a high feature cardinality?

options.text: The model's performance becomes more predictable across different data distributions.
options.rationale: High feature cardinality leads to unpredictable performance due to the exponential growth of combinations.

options.text: The number of possible combinations of features grows exponentially, leading to unpredictable performance.
options.rationale: A high cardinality of features causes the number of combinations to grow exponentially, making performance unpredictable.

options.text: The model requires fewer access layers for security purposes.
options.rationale: The text suggests that models should have several access layers for security, not fewer.

questions.explanation: Models with high feature cardinality are more sensitive to data issues because the exponential growth in the number of possible feature combinations can lead to unpredictable performance.
hints: Consider the relationship between feature count and combination growth.
Focus on the impact of high cardinality on model stability.

questions.stem: In the context of model deployment risks, what is a potential issue associated with a model having a high cardinality of features?

options.text: The model becomes less sensitive to changes in production data.

options.text: The model requires less computational power during inference.
options.rationale: The text does not mention computational power as a consequence of high cardinality.

options.text: The performance of the model may become unpredictable due to the exponential increase in feature combinations.
options.rationale: The exponential number of combinations in high cardinality models can lead to unpredictable performance.

options.text: The model is immune to data leakage risks associated with external environment connections.
options.rationale: The text discusses data leakage risks separately from the issue of high feature cardinality.

questions.stem: What is a potential risk associated with a model predicting sensitive information, such as salary, using a nearest neighbor algorithm?

options.text: The model might fail due to seasonality events.
options.rationale: Seasonality events relate to dynamic thresholds or anomaly detection, not directly to data leakage from the model's prediction mechanism.

options.text: The model might reveal sensitive information if certain input features are known.
options.rationale: A model can leak data if knowing specific input features allows one to deduce sensitive information, such as salary.

options.text: The model's performance will become unpredictable due to low feature cardinality.
options.rationale: High feature cardinality is what makes a model more sensitive to issues, not low cardinality.

options.text: The model will always require dynamic thresholds for accurate output.
options.rationale: Dynamic thresholds are used in classification problems to handle seasonality, not as a universal requirement for all models.

questions.explanation: Data leakage occurs when a model reveals sensitive information based on the input features provided, such as when a nearest neighbor model predicts salary and the user knows the zip code, age, and profession of a person.
hints: Consider the scenario where a model predicts a sensitive value.
Think about what information, when combined with the model's output, causes the leak.

questions.stem: When monitoring a model in production, what is one proactive measure to address feature values that fall outside of expected ranges?

options.text: CI/CD pipeline failure
options.rationale: CI/CD pipeline failure is related to the deployment process, not specifically external environmental risks.

options.text: Hyperparameter misconfiguration

questions.stem: What is a primary benefit of using containers regarding software deployment?

options.text: They automatically handle all hardware maintenance tasks.
options.rationale: Containers focus on packaging applications and dependencies, not managing hardware maintenance.

options.text: They ensure that the application runs reliably across different computing environments.

options.text: They eliminate the need for any local installation of packages on the host machine.
options.rationale: While they reduce local installation needs, they are tools for packaging, not a complete replacement for all environment setup.

options.rationale: A container packages code and all its dependencies so the application runs reliably across environments.

questions.stem: What is the fundamental role of the Dockerfile in the process of containerization?

questions.stem: What is the primary benefit of Docker images being immutable?

options.rationale: Immutability means the image cannot be changed after it is produced.

options.text: It guarantees that experimentation in one environment will not impact the image in another environment.

options.text: It permits the addition of new commands to the image after the initial build process is complete.

options.text: It makes the process of debugging significantly more complex.

options.text: Images are snapshots taken at a specific time and place.
options.rationale: An image is a snapshot of a Docker virtual machine at a certain time and place.

options.text: Images are designed to interact with other processes sharing the same kernel.

questions.explanation: Docker images are snapshots, which means they represent a fixed state at a certain time and place. This immutability is key to easier debugging because the environment remains consistent.

questions.stem: Which command specifies the default command to be executed inside a container?

options.rationale: The RUN command is used to build the container by executing commands during the image creation process.

options.text: CMD
options.rationale: The CMD command specifies what command should run within the container when it starts.

options.text: EXPOSE
options.rationale: The EXPOSE command is used to configure how a container listens for connections, but it is not the command that runs the application.

options.text: FROM
options.rationale: The FROM command specifies the base image from which the new image is created.

questions.explanation: The CMD instruction in a Dockerfile defines the primary process that the container will execute upon startup.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of using a.dockerignore file when building a Docker image?

options.text: To define the sequence of instructions used in the Dockerfile.
options.rationale: The Dockerfile defines the instructions, whereas.dockerignore specifies what to exclude from the build context.

options.text: To prevent large or sensitive files and directories from being sent to the daemon or included in the image.
options.rationale: The file modifies the context to exclude matching files and directories from being sent to the daemon or added to the image.

options.text: To automatically manage cache invalidations between different build layers.
options.rationale: Cache invalidations are related to the Dockerfile instructions, not the.dockerignore file.

options.text: To specify which files must be copied using the COPY instruction.
options.rationale: The COPY instruction in the Dockerfile specifies which files are copied, not the.dockerignore file.

questions.explanation: The.dockerignore file is used to specify patterns that exclude files and directories from the Docker build context, thereby preventing unnecessary or sensitive data from being sent to the daemon or included in the final image.

questions.stem: What is the primary benefit of ordering instructions in a Dockerfile from least to most frequently changed?

options.text: It ensures that the final image size is always minimized.
options.rationale: Image size minimization is not the direct benefit of instruction ordering, but rather related to file selection.

options.text: It allows for more effective cache utilization during subsequent builds.
options.rationale: Placing less frequently changed instructions later allows Docker to reuse cached layers for earlier, more stable instructions.

options.text: It guarantees that sensitive files are never included in the final image.

options.text: It forces the Docker daemon to ignore all previous build steps.

questions.stem: If a Dockerfile contains multiple statements, what is the consequence regarding image building and resource usage?

options.text: Each statement results in a single, monolithic image layer that is highly efficient.

options.text: The process is optimized because Docker automatically merges all subsequent instructions into one layer.
options.rationale: Docker does not automatically merge all instructions into one layer; each instruction creates a new layer.

options.text: Each statement generates a new intermediate image layer, potentially causing cache invalidations and wasting resources.
options.rationale: Every statement in the Dockerfile results in building a new intermediate image layer, which can cause resource wastage.

options.text: The use of multiple statements is only beneficial when using Docker Compose for orchestration.
options.rationale: The layering effect is inherent to the Dockerfile structure, regardless of whether Compose is used.

options.text: It is typically triggered by a user action, such as clicking 'checkout'.
options.rationale: Real-Time Inference is associated with an instant trigger from a user action.

options.text: The critical performance metric is throughput.
options.rationale: Throughput is critical for Batch Inference, whereas Real-Time Inference prioritizes speed.

options.text: The required response time is measured in milliseconds.
options.rationale: Real-Time Inference requires speed, meaning its response time is measured in milliseconds.

questions.explanation: Real-Time Inference is characterized by instant triggering from user actions and a critical need for speed, measured in milliseconds, unlike batch processing which focuses on throughput.

questions.stem: When deploying models, what is a primary benefit of deploying new models alongside stable ones?

options.text: It guarantees the new model will perform better than the stable model.

options.text: It allows for gradual traffic shifting to test the new model's performance.
options.rationale: Deploying new models alongside stable ones allows for gradual traffic shifting, such as in canary releases, to monitor performance.

options.text: It reduces the overall resource utilization required by the serving infrastructure.
options.rationale: Deploying alongside stable models may require maintaining resources for both versions.

options.text: It eliminates the need for any traffic management layer, like a Load Balancer.
options.rationale: Model deployment infrastructure often includes components like Load Balancers to manage traffic.

questions.explanation: Deploying new models alongside stable ones enables strategies like canary releases, which allow for gradual traffic shifting to monitor the new model's performance before a full rollout.

questions.stem: When implementing shadow testing, what is the primary characteristic of the challenger model's operation relative to the live system?

options.rationale: The challenger model must run alongside the champion model using the identical input data.

options.text: It is only used when the champion model is completely offline for maintenance.
options.rationale: Shadow testing is a mandatory practice for ensuring safety and unbiased comparison while running alongside the champion.

options.text: Its primary goal is to reduce the resource utilization of the serving platform.
options.rationale: The purpose of shadow testing is to ensure safety and unbiased comparison, not primarily resource reduction.

questions.explanation: Shadow testing mandates that the challenger model runs alongside the champion model using the same input data, but its predictions do not impact the live system, which is crucial for safety and unbiased comparison.

questions.stem: Under what circumstances is shadow testing not possible?

options.text: When the ground truth can be easily evaluated for both models.
options.rationale: If ground truth can be evaluated, it does not present the workload issue that prevents shadow testing.

options.text: When the objective to optimize is only indirectly related to the prediction performance.
options.rationale: Shadow testing is impossible if the optimization objective is indirectly related to the prediction's performance, such as using buy rate for an ad click prediction.

options.text: When the workload remains constant even with two models running.
options.rationale: The total number of predictions does not change, but this doesn't negate the impossibility conditions listed.

options.text: When obtaining ground truth requires an excessive workload, like contacting all clients for fraud detection.
options.rationale: If obtaining ground truth for both models creates too much workload, shadow testing is not possible.

options.text: When the prediction is a simple binary classification.
options.rationale: The text provides examples involving complex predictions like recommendation lists, not simple binary ones.

questions.explanation: Shadow testing fails when the workload for ground truth evaluation becomes too high, or when the metric used for optimization is not directly tied to the model's prediction output.
hints: Look for the two distinct scenarios listed where shadow testing cannot be performed.

questions.stem: Under what condition is shadow testing generally considered more expensive than A/B testing?

options.text: When the objective is directly related to the prediction performance.
options.rationale: Shadow testing is more expensive because it uses multiple models for inferencing on the same data, increasing resource consumption.

options.text: When the ground truth can be easily evaluated for both models.
options.rationale: The expense is related to running multiple models, not just the ease of ground truth evaluation.

options.text: When multiple models are used for inferencing on the same data.
options.rationale: Using multiple models for inferencing on the same data increases resource consumption, making shadow testing more expensive.

options.text: When the workload remains constant regardless of the number of models.
options.rationale: Shadow testing increases workload because it runs multiple models, which is not constant.

questions.explanation: Shadow testing is inherently more expensive because it requires running multiple models simultaneously on the same data, which increases the consumption of resources like memory and processing power.
hints: Review the comparison between A/B testing and shadow testing regarding resource consumption.

questions.stem: Which of the following represent disadvantages or limitations associated with A/B testing?

options.text: It necessitates careful data splitting to prevent sampling bias.
options.rationale: A/B testing requires careful data splitting to avoid sampling bias, which is a requirement/disadvantage.

options.text: It is generally simpler to implement compared to shadow testing.
options.rationale: The text implies A/B testing is often harder to implement than shadow testing, not simpler.

options.text: It can introduce risks if the model being tested experiences performance degradation.
options.rationale: A/B testing carries risks if the tested model degrades.

options.text: It always results in a lower overall cost due to its efficient resource use.
options.rationale: Using multiple models increases resource consumption, which indirectly increases cost.

options.text: It is inherently impossible to manage the workload increase associated with testing.
options.rationale: The text discusses workload increases in the context of shadow testing limitations, not as a universal, inherent impossibility of A/B testing.

questions.explanation: A/B testing requires careful data splitting to manage bias, and it carries risks if the tested model degrades. Conversely, it increases resource consumption and is often harder to implement than shadow testing.

questions.stem: Which of the following are examples of changes that can constitute data drift?

questions.stem: When assessing the difference between two discrete distributions, what is the primary purpose of using a measure like the Population Stability Index (PSI)?

options.text: It allows for comparison regardless of the order of the reference and comparison distributions.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements accurately describe the role of the KS test in the context of distribution comparison?

questions.stem: What is the fundamental limitation of univariate drift detection?

options.text: It cannot detect changes in the mean of any single feature.

options.text: It fails when the relationship between features changes, even if individual distributions look standard.

options.text: It is only effective for high-dimensional datasets.

questions.stem: What is the primary mechanism used by a multivariate approach, such as PCA, to detect changes in feature distributions?

options.text: By analyzing the variance of each feature in isolation.

options.text: By checking for shifts in the standard deviation of the features.

questions.stem: Which of the following steps are involved in using a multivariate approach, like PCA, to detect covariate shift?

options.text: Identifying directions that capture the most variance in the data.
options.rationale: Dimensionality reduction involves identifying directions containing the most variance.

options.text: Learning the PCA mapping from a designated reference period.
options.rationale: A reference period must be chosen to train the PCA mapping before applying it to new data.

options.text: Ensuring the data compression is perfectly lossless.
options.rationale: The process involves lossy compression, which contributes to the expected reconstruction error.

options.text: Monitoring the deviation of the reconstruction error from expected ranges.
options.rationale: Monitoring reconstruction error against expected ranges is the mechanism for detecting the shift.

questions.stem: What is the fundamental mechanism ADWIN employs to detect drift?

options.text: It analyzes the distribution of the entire incoming dataset to find anomalies.

options.text: It compares the means of two distinct sub-windows within the incoming data stream.

questions.stem: What distinguishes concept drift from covariate shift and label drift?

options.text: Concept drift is solely related to changes in the input feature distribution.
options.rationale: Concept drift is related to the relationship between input features and the target label, not just the data distribution.

options.text: Concept drift is related to the relationship between the input features and the target label.
options.rationale: Concept drift is defined by a change in the relationship between input features (x) and the target label (y), denoted as P(y|x).

options.text: Concept drift only occurs due to temporary fluctuations in the data collection process.
options.rationale: While temporary changes can explain it, concept drift is mainly a result of changes in the real-world phenomena being modeled.

options.text: Concept drift is always detected using the Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN) algorithm.
options.rationale: The text mentions ADWIN as a method for detecting drift, but it does not state that it is the only or defining characteristic of concept drift.

questions.stem: If a model's performance degrades because the underlying real-world process it models has fundamentally changed its behavior, this degradation is best classified as:

options.text: Covariate Shift
options.rationale: Covariate shift relates to changes in the input data distribution, not the relationship between inputs and outputs.

options.text: Label Drift
options.rationale: Label drift relates to changes in the class distribution, not the relationship between inputs and outputs.

options.text: Concept Drift
options.rationale: Concept drift specifically describes changes in the relationship between the input features and the target label due to real-world changes.

options.text: Data Bias
options.rationale: Data bias is a potential cause, but the fundamental change in the relationship is termed concept drift.

questions.explanation: When the relationship P(y/x) changes because the real-world phenomenon itself changes, this is the definition of concept drift, distinguishing it from shifts in input or output distributions alone.

questions.stem: In the Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN) algorithm, what triggers the generation of an alarm indicating drift?

options.text: The total number of data points processed exceeds a fixed limit.
options.rationale: The alarm is triggered when the absolute difference between two means exceeds a pre-defined threshold, not just by volume.

options.text: The sliding window size reaches its maximum predefined limit.
options.rationale: ADWIN uses a sliding window approach, but the alarm condition is based on mean differences, not window size limits.

options.text: The absolute difference between the means of two distinct sub-windows exceeds a user-defined threshold.
options.rationale: An alarm is generated if the absolute difference between the two means derived from two sub-windows exceeds the pre-defined threshold.

options.text: The system detects that the data distribution has shifted significantly.
options.rationale: While ADWIN detects change, the specific trigger for the alarm mentioned is the threshold breach between sub-window means.

questions.explanation: The ADWIN algorithm generates an alarm when the absolute difference between the means calculated from two sub-windows surpasses a threshold set by the user.
hints: Focus on the specific condition that causes the 'alarm' in ADWIN.

questions.stem: Which concept drift handling strategy is suitable when the drift is abrupt?

options.rationale: Passive updates are effective for gradual drifts and recurring concepts, not abrupt ones.

options.text: Active detection
options.rationale: The active strategy is designed to update the model only when a drift is detected, which works well for abrupt drifts.

options.text: Data weighting
options.rationale: Data weighting is a variation of updating the model by giving more importance to recent data.

options.text: Ensemble methods
options.rationale: Ensemble methods combine an old model with a new one to capture different patterns.

questions.explanation: Active detection is the appropriate strategy when the concept drift is abrupt because it triggers an update only upon detection of the drift.

options.rationale: The rule states that a container should have the same lifetime as the app, but separating services allows for horizontal scaling.

options.text: It makes the debugging process significantly more difficult.
options.rationale: Using a container for each process makes debugging and orchestration easier.

options.text: It allows for horizontal scaling of a specific service to handle increased traffic.
options.rationale: A separate container for each service enables the system to grow one web server horizontally to accommodate extra traffic.

options.text: It forces all services to use unique, versioned tags for deployment.
options.rationale: While unique tags are good practice, the direct benefit of service separation is related to scaling.

questions.explanation: By isolating each service into its own container, you gain the flexibility to scale that specific service independently (horizontally) when traffic demands it.
hints: Think about how different services can be managed independently.
What does the text say about growing a web server?

questions.stem: If an organization wants to extend its production cluster to many nodes while ensuring that each node pulls a specific, unchanging version of an image, what Docker practice should they implement?

options.text: Use a single, universally recognized tag like ':latest' for all deployments.
options.rationale: Using a single tag like ':latest' risks inconsistencies because the tag changes frequently.

options.text: Define all services using Docker Compose without specifying image tags.
options.rationale: Not specifying tags prevents the system from knowing which version to pull.

options.text: Utilize unique tags for each deployment to prevent inconsistencies across hosts.
options.rationale: Using unique tags prevents inconsistencies and ensures hosts do not fetch any other docker image version.

options.text: Ensure every container runs as its own process, regardless of the application structure.
options.rationale: While running one container per process is good, it does not guarantee version consistency across nodes.

questions.explanation: To prevent inconsistencies when extending a cluster across numerous nodes, one must use unique tags for deployments, ensuring that each host fetches the intended, specific image version.
hints: Focus on the requirement: preventing inconsistencies across multiple nodes.

questions.stem: A scheduled process, like running a prediction at 2:00 AM every Sunday, best fits which category of inference?

options.rationale: Online inference requires an instant response, whereas a scheduled process runs offline.

options.text: Batch Inference

questions.stem: Which of the following deployment characteristics or examples are specifically associated with Online Inference?

options.text: The requirement to block a credit card transaction immediately.
options.rationale: Credit card fraud detection requires immediate blocking, which fits the low-latency requirement of Online Inference.

options.text: A scheduled process running every Sunday morning.
options.rationale: Scheduled processes are characteristic of Batch Inference, not Online Inference.

options.text: The need for the system to respond within milliseconds.
options.rationale: Online Inference is defined by its critical need for speed, measured in milliseconds.

options.text: Processing millions of observations all at once.
options.rationale: Processing millions of observations simultaneously is the definition of Batch Inference.

options.text: The use of APIs and Load Balancers to handle real-time requests.
options.rationale: Online Inference requires infrastructure like APIs and Load Balancers to manage immediate, high-availability requests.

questions.explanation: Online Inference is tied to immediate user actions (like checkout or fraud detection) demanding millisecond response times, and it requires robust infrastructure like APIs and Load Balancers to handle this real-time load.

questions.stem: Which of the following are recognized consequences or aspects related to data drift?

questions.stem: If a multivariate approach is required to detect covariate shift, what is the primary goal of using dimensionality reduction techniques like PCA?

options.text: To ensure that the reconstruction error is always zero.
options.rationale: Dimensionality reduction is a lossy compression process, meaning the reconstruction error will not be zero.

options.text: To identify the directions in the data that contain the most variance.
options.rationale: Dimensionality reduction aims to find the directions that capture the maximum amount of variance in the data.

options.text: To eliminate all correlations between the features before reconstruction.
options.rationale: While related to variance, the goal is variance capture, not necessarily complete decorrelation before reconstruction.

options.text: To guarantee that the data compression process is perfectly lossless.
options.rationale: The process is described as a lossy compression process, which inherently introduces reconstruction error.

questions.explanation: Dimensionality reduction, such as PCA, works by identifying the principal components—the directions in the data that account for the greatest amount of variance.
hints: What is the purpose of identifying 'directions of our data that contain most of the variance'?
Think about how PCA summarizes complex data into fewer dimensions.

questions.stem: A multivariate approach using PCA can detect covariate shift even when:

options.text: The marginal univariate distribution of each feature remains standard normal.
options.rationale: The text shows an example where marginal distributions are standard normal, but correlations change, requiring a multivariate approach.

options.text: The features are negatively correlated in the production data compared to the reference.
options.rationale: The example provided shows a shift from positive to negative correlation, which is captured by the multivariate method.

options.text: The data is perfectly compressed without any information loss.
options.rationale: The process is lossy, meaning perfect compression is not achieved.

options.text: The shift only affects the mean of a single feature.
options.rationale: Univariate tests are designed to catch single-feature mean shifts, but the multivariate approach is needed for joint feature changes.

questions.explanation: The primary advantage of the multivariate approach is its ability to detect shifts in the joint feature space, such as changes in correlation, even when the individual features maintain their standard normal marginal distributions.
hints: Look at the specific examples given regarding feature distributions and correlations.
Contrast the capabilities of univariate vs. multivariate methods.

questions.stem: What is a limitation of using univariate statistical tests for drift detection?

options.text: They are too slow for real-time monitoring.
options.rationale: The text does not mention speed as a limitation of univariate tests.

options.text: They treat each feature in isolation from the rest.
options.rationale: Univariate statistical tests analyze each feature independently, ignoring relationships with other features.

options.text: They require a multivariate approach to function correctly.
options.rationale: Univariate tests are a specific type of test, and the text describes their limitation, not their requirement for multivariate methods.

options.text: They are only applicable when features are normally distributed.
options.rationale: The text does not state that univariate tests are restricted only to normally distributed features.

questions.explanation: Univariate statistical tests are inherently limited because they examine each feature separately, failing to account for how features interact with one another.
hints: Consider how 'univariate' relates to analyzing multiple variables.

questions.stem: When does the older sub-window get dropped during the ADWIN process?

options.text: When the total number of incoming data points reaches a predefined maximum limit.
options.rationale: The older sub-window is dropped when two sub-windows show distinct means in the new observations.

options.text: When the absolute difference between the two means falls below the user-defined threshold.
options.rationale: The older sub-window is dropped when two sub-windows show distinct means, which is related to exceeding the threshold.

options.text: When the means of two sub-windows exhibit a significant difference in the new observations.
options.rationale: The older sub-window is dropped when two sub-windows show distinct means in the new observations.

options.text: When the monitoring system detects a change in the label distribution.
options.rationale: Concept drift is not related to the class distribution, which is different from the conditions under which the window is dropped.

questions.explanation: The older sub-window is dropped when two sub-windows show distinct means in the new observations.
hints: Think about the condition that triggers the dropping of the older segment.
The comparison of means between sub-windows is key to this action.

questions.stem: Under what condition is concept drift mathematically represented as occurring?

options.text: When the distribution of input features changes between training and testing.
options.rationale: A change in input feature distribution is related to covariate shift, not the definition of concept drift.

options.text: When the conditional probability of the target label given the input features differs between training and testing sets.
options.rationale: Concept drift is indicated when P_train(y/x) is not equal to P_test(y/x).

options.text: When the class distribution shifts, causing an imbalance in the target labels.
options.rationale: Concept drift is explicitly stated to be different from label drift, which relates to class distribution changes.

options.text: When the sliding window algorithm fails to detect any change in the data stream.
options.rationale: The detection mechanism (like ADWIN) is a tool, not the definition of the drift itself.

questions.explanation: Concept drift occurs when the underlying relationship, P(y|x), changes, meaning the probability of observing a specific label given the features is different between the training and testing environments.
hints: Look for the mathematical notation that defines the change in the relationship.

questions.stem: If a Generative AI model begins producing outputs that are noticeably worse or degraded, which type of drift is most likely occurring?

options.text: Prompt Drift
options.rationale: Prompt Drift relates to changes in the input topics, not the quality of the output.

options.text: Concept/Task Drift
options.rationale: Concept/Task Drift is tied to changes in external business rules, not necessarily output degradation alone.

options.text: Behavioral Drift
options.rationale: Behavioral Drift is defined as a degradation in the model's output quality.

options.text: Input Drift
options.rationale: Input Drift is not the specific term used to describe output degradation in this context.

questions.explanation: Behavioral Drift specifically describes the phenomenon where the model's output quality degrades over time.
hints: Focus on the word 'degradation' in the question.
Relate this to how the model is performing, not what the user is asking.

questions.stem: If a model's output contains a factual or logical error, which error type is this classified as, and what is its primary root cause?

options.text: Data Drift; caused by real-world changes in the input data.
options.rationale: Data Drift is a distribution mismatch, not a factual/logic error.

options.text: Hallucination; caused by the model's architecture or training constraints.
options.rationale: Hallucination is defined as a factual/logic error stemming from model architecture or training constraints.

options.text: Data Drift; caused by a mismatch in the input data's distribution.
options.rationale: This describes Data Drift, but the error type described (factual/logic) is Hallucination.

options.text: Hallucination; caused by external covariate shifts in the production environment.
options.rationale: Covariate/Concept shift is the root cause associated with Data Drift, not Hallucination.

questions.explanation: A factual or logic error is defined as Hallucination, and its root cause is attributed to the model's architecture or training constraints.
hints: Recall the definition of Hallucination.
What causes Hallucination?

questions.stem: Which of the following are valid reasons or indicators for monitoring in an MLOps feedback loop?

options.text: Detecting feature changes between training and production
options.rationale: Detecting feature changes is one method to identify pipeline breaks or data problems.

options.text: Tracking the model's internal mathematical precision
options.rationale: The goal is not to obsess over perfecting the mathematics of catching data changes.

options.text: Detecting prediction distribution shifts between production periods
options.rationale: Shifts in prediction distribution can serve as a proxy for performance changes, especially when ground truth is delayed.

options.text: Identifying clusters of new data that are problematic for the model
options.rationale: Data discovery involves finding new data to label or problematic clusters in unstructured data.

options.text: Ensuring the model perfectly matches its initial training data distribution
options.rationale: Models often extend well to modified data, so perfect matching is not the primary goal of drift metrics.

questions.explanation: Monitoring involves detecting feature changes, prediction distribution shifts, and identifying problematic data clusters. The focus is on practical stability rather than mathematical perfection.
hints: Review the bullet points listed under '04: Monitoring and feedback loop'.
What are the practical applications of drift metrics?

questions.stem: If a monitoring system detects a sudden, immediate error in the model's output that is a factual mistake, this issue is most likely related to:

options.text: A concept shift occurring gradually.
options.rationale: A concept shift is a gradual, temporal change, whereas the error described is instantaneous.

options.text: A pipeline break during data transformation.
options.rationale: While pipeline breaks can be detected, the description points to a logical/factual error, which is a hallucination.

options.text: A hallucination stemming from model constraints.
options.rationale: A factual/logic error that happens instantly aligns with the definition and nature of a hallucination.

options.text: A change in feature distribution between training and production.
options.rationale: Feature distribution changes relate to Data Drift, which is a distribution mismatch, not necessarily an instantaneous logic error.

questions.explanation: A factual or logic error that can happen instantly is the definition of a hallucination, which is rooted in model architecture or training constraints.
hints: Match the 'instantaneous factual/logic error' to its primary nature.
Recall the root causes associated with that primary nature.

questions.stem: Which of the following activities are part of the monitoring and feedback loop in MLOps?

options.text: Detecting feature changes between training and production.
options.rationale: Detecting feature changes is a method to detect pipeline breaks or problems transforming data.

options.text: Using drift metrics solely to perfect the mathematical detection of data changes.
options.rationale: The text advises against obsessing over perfecting the mathematics of catching data changes in practice.

options.text: Identifying new data clusters that are problematic for the model in unstructured data.
options.rationale: Data discovery involves identifying new data to label or clusters of new data that are problematic for the model.

options.text: Ensuring the model's architecture is perfectly stable regardless of production changes.
options.rationale: Many models extend well to modified data, suggesting stability is not the sole focus.

options.text: Using drift as a signal to determine when and how often to retrain the model.
options.rationale: Drift is a signal for when to retrain and how often to retrain.

questions.explanation: The monitoring loop includes detecting feature changes, identifying problematic new data via data discovery, and using drift as a trigger for retraining. Over-focusing on mathematical perfection is discouraged.
hints: Review the bullet points describing the monitoring and feedback loop.
Consider the practical advice given in the 'Take home lesson' sections.

questions.stem: Which of the following actions are related to gaining insight into a model's behavior in the real world?

options.text: Only assessing the model's performance in low-risk production environments.

questions.stem: Which of the following characteristics are associated with the Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM)?

questions.stem: The sum of the Shapley values for all features in a model equals the difference between the model's prediction for the instance and the expected prediction.

options.text: True
options.rationale: The sum of all Shapley values ($\sum \phi_j$) equals the model's prediction ($\hat{f}(x)$) minus the expected prediction ($E$).

options.text: False
options.rationale: The sum of the Shapley values equals the difference between the prediction and the expected value.

questions.explanation: It is a property that the sum of the Shapley values for all features equals the difference between the model's prediction for the specific instance ($\hat{f}(x)$) and the expected prediction over the entire feature space ($E$).
hints: Consider the equation relating the sum of $\phi_j$ to $\hat{f}(x)$ and $E$.
This property ensures that the contributions account for the total deviation from the average.

questions.stem: What potential issue can arise when conducting A/B testing in production?

options.text: Model overfitting during the testing phase.
options.rationale: Overfitting is a training issue, not a direct consequence of the A/B testing methodology itself.

options.text: The introduction of sampling bias.
options.rationale: A/B testing in production can introduce sampling bias when traffic is split between models.

options.text: Inability to measure the model's prediction latency.
options.rationale: Latency measurement is a separate performance metric, not a bias introduced by the testing setup.

options.text: Complete failure of the champion model during the test.
options.rationale: While failure is possible, sampling bias is a specific methodological challenge mentioned in the concept.

questions.explanation: A key challenge when running A/B tests in production is the risk of sampling bias, which can affect the fairness of the comparison between the two models.
hints: Consider how traffic is divided between the two models.
Bias relates to how representative the sample is of the whole population.

questions.stem: In a scenario where new models are tested against a champion model while in production, what is a key benefit of shadow testing?

options.rationale: Shadow testing is characterized by testing against the same input, which avoids sampling bias.

options.text: It conserves resources by reducing consumption during testing.
options.rationale: The text does not state that shadow testing saves on resource consumption.

options.text: It is safer because testing occurs against identical inputs, eliminating sampling bias on target events.
options.rationale: Shadow testing is safer because it tests against the same input, meaning there is no room for sampling bias on target events.

options.text: It enables the use of different statistical protocols for model comparison.
options.rationale: Shadow testing focuses on identical inputs rather than requiring different statistical protocols across subpopulations.

questions.explanation: Shadow testing is advantageous because it tests against the same input, making it safer as there is no room for sampling bias on the target events.
hints: Consider the nature of input data when comparing challenger and champion models in shadow testing.

options.rationale: The Automated Training Pipeline is concerned with data fetching and model registration, not the final production deployment.

options.text: Deploy Pipeline
options.rationale: The Deploy Pipeline is the pipeline for putting the model into production.

options.text: Model Registry
options.rationale: The Model Registry tracks versions of the model, which is a prerequisite but not the deployment action itself.

questions.explanation: The Deploy Pipeline is the dedicated component within Continuous Model Delivery whose purpose is to design and execute the process of putting the model into a production environment.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements are integral components or features within the Continuous Model Delivery framework?

options.text: A Feature Store providing preprocessed data.
options.rationale: The Feature Store is the first component, making data accessible to all teams.

options.text: A system where engineers manually integrate models into applications.
options.rationale: Manual integration is characteristic of the older, non-continuous implementation, not the modern framework.

options.text: An Automated Training pipeline interfacing with the Feature Store.
options.rationale: The Automated Training pipeline is described as launching from the Deploy Pipeline or a Trigger and using the Feature Store.

options.text: A requirement for software engineers to handle all monitoring tasks.
options.rationale: In the manual setup, the DevOps team handles monitoring, but this is not a defining component of the continuous delivery structure itself.

options.text: A Modularized code output from the model building phase.
options.rationale: Automated Model Building and Analysis outputs Modularized code to standardize and automate subsequent steps.

questions.explanation: Continuous Model Delivery relies on structured components like the Feature Store, Automated Model Building, and the Deploy Pipeline, which includes the Automated Training pipeline.

questions.stem: If an organization needs to ensure that a model deployment works identically whether it is run on a developer's laptop or on a production server, which capability of containers is most relevant?

options.text: Isolation of use cases
options.rationale: Isolation helps manage different versions, but reproducibility is key for identical environments across different machines.

options.text: Light resource consumption
options.rationale: Light resource consumption is an efficiency benefit, not the primary mechanism for environment consistency.

options.text: Portability
options.rationale: Portability ensures that the containerized application runs reliably from one computing environment to another.

options.text: Hardware abstraction
options.rationale: Hardware abstraction is characteristic of virtual machines, which containers aim to address.

questions.explanation: Portability is the feature that allows a container to function consistently across different computing environments, satisfying the requirement for identical operation between development and production.
hints: What term describes the ability to run the same thing in different places?

questions.stem: Images are immutable, meaning that once an image is created, it cannot be altered.

options.text: True

options.text: False

questions.explanation: Images are immutable snapshots; any modification requires creating a new image.

questions.stem: A Docker image is defined as:

options.text: A dynamic state that can be altered after its creation to accommodate new features.
options.rationale: Images are immutable, meaning they cannot be changed after they are produced.

options.text: A snapshot of a Docker virtual machine at a specific point in time and location.
options.rationale: An image is defined as a snapshot of a Docker virtual machine at a certain time and place.

questions.explanation: An image serves as a fixed snapshot of a virtual machine state, which is why it is immutable.
hints: Focus on the definition provided for an image.
Relate 'snapshot' to the concept of changeability.

questions.stem: A Dockerfile's purpose is to contain all the necessary instructions for Docker to automatically build a specific image.

options.text: False

questions.stem: If a developer wants to ensure their application listens for incoming connections on a specific port, which Dockerfile command is used for this configuration?

options.text: RUN
options.rationale: The RUN command is used to execute commands during the image build process, not to configure listening ports.

options.text: CMD
options.rationale: The CMD command specifies the default command to run inside the container.

options.text: ENV
options.rationale: The ENV command is used to set environment variables, not to define network listening ports.

options.text: EXPOSE

questions.stem: The.dockerignore file is checked by the Docker build process before any Dockerfile instructions are executed.

options.text: True

options.text: False

questions.explanation: The Docker build process scans for the.dockerignore file in the root directory of the context, and if found, it modifies the context to exclude specified files and directories.

questions.stem: Which of the following scenarios or practices align with recommended Docker best practices?

options.text: Using official or trusted images as the base for new containers.
options.rationale: Using official or trusted images is a good practice.

options.text: Placing all application processes into a single, monolithic container for simplicity.
options.rationale: A best practice is to use a dedicated container for each process, not consolidate them all.

options.text: Leveraging Docker's built-in caching mechanisms during the build phase.
options.rationale: Leveraging Docker caches is a good practice to improve efficiency.

options.text: Managing complex, multi-container applications using Docker Compose.
options.rationale: Docker Compose is recommended for managing applications that consist of multiple containers.

options.text: Including all source code and configuration files in the build context without filtering.
options.rationale: This practice risks exposing sensitive data and unnecessarily increasing build time, which is contrary to best practices.

questions.explanation: Best practices involve using trusted sources, isolating processes, optimizing builds via caching, and using tools like Docker Compose for complexity.
hints: Check the summary for the four core recommended practices.
Consider the trade-off between simplicity and security/efficiency.

questions.stem: A separate container for each service is recommended because it allows for horizontal scaling of individual services.

options.text: True
options.rationale: Using a separate container for each service enables the growth of one web server horizontally to handle increased traffic.

options.text: False
options.rationale: Using separate containers allows for horizontal scaling, making the statement true.

questions.explanation: The practice of using a separate container for each service specifically allows you to scale one service horizontally to accommodate extra traffic.

questions.stem: Using specific, unique tags for deployments is recommended because it prevents inconsistencies across different hosts in the production cluster.

options.text: True
options.rationale: Using unique tags prevents inconsistencies and ensures hosts fetch the exact intended docker image version.

options.text: False
options.rationale: Using unique tags is a good practice to prevent inconsistencies, not a bad one.

questions.explanation: Unique tags are crucial for production stability because they lock the image version, preventing hosts from unexpectedly pulling different versions.
